meta.vintage: Stats 2025 season; staff/QB notes web-verified 2026-07-13 (top 25 + full SEC)

Indiana.ob: Zone-RPO spread under Curt Cignetti — 11 personnel, condensed splits, heavy play-action and glance RPOs off wide zone.
Indiana.ot: Fresh off a 16-0 NATIONAL CHAMPIONSHIP — the efficiency machine finished the job. Methodical by design: elite success rate with little vertical variance, best red-zone offense in the country. QB room resets after Fernando Mendoza's departure — the system, not the passer, is the constant.
Indiana.db: 4-2-5 under Bryant Haines — aggressive simulated pressures, run-through linebackers, press-quarters behind it.
Indiana.dt: #1 stuff rate and #2 havoc in FBS: they win at the line and on early downs. The tradeoff is boom-bust — #108 at preventing explosives. Teams that protect and take shots have a path; nobody grinds them down.
s: Best situational football in the sport — red zone and 3rd down on both sides
Coaching edge: Cignetti's staff wins the margins
w: Explosiveness allergy on offense caps blowout upside vs elite defenses
New QB after Mendoza — efficiency machine needs a new trigger man
Secondary can be beaten over the top when pressure doesn't land

deep.intro: 2025: 16-0. National champions. First in SP+. Beat Ohio State in Indy, beat Oregon twice, beat Miami for the title. 2026: Cignetti is 27-2 in Bloomington. SP+: #5, 9.9 avg wins (7.0 conference). Vegas: 10.5 total, +260.
deep.roster: Gone: Mendoza (No. 1 overall pick), two 1,000-yard rushers, two 800-yard receivers, eight draft picks. Cignetti's answer was a portal class Connelly likes better than last year's — a sentence he calls scary. QB Josh Hoover (TCU) arrives with 9,629 career passing yards and 71 TDs — more proven than Mendoza was at the same point. RB Turbo Richard, WRs Nick Marsh (Michigan State — scouts' darling) and Shazz Preston (Tulane's top big-play threat on a CFP team), Wisconsin's best lineman (Joe Brunner), three transfer edges with elite pressure rates (Chiddi Obiazor, Tobi Osunsanmi, Josh Burnham), and FCS-champion corner Carson Williams. The internal star: WR Charlie Becker, who averaged an outrageous 3.7 yards per route over the season's final two months — better than Jeremiah Smith's full-season number — with game-winning catches in IU's three tightest wins. The trenches keep All-American LT Carter Smith, guards Bray Lynch and Drew Evans, DTs Tyrique Tucker and Mario Landino, and semifinal breakout DE Daniel Ndukwe. Watch list: Hoover (interception habit is the one red flag — Cignetti has already had to publicly defend him), Becker, the Becker/Marsh/Preston trio.
deep.identity: Offense: Mike Shanahan's precision spread — the scheme made a Heisman winner out of a Cal transfer; now it gets a more experienced arm. Defense: Bryant Haines' aggressive structure returns proven interior anchors but needs the transfer edges to hit, because line play has powered this entire two-year run. Strengths: the best coaching staff in the sport by results, scouting-and-development edge, portal precision, house-money-turned-empire culture. Weaknesses: 52nd in returning production (normal for champions, abnormal for Indiana), Hoover's turnovers, and the target on their back — every opponent's Super Bowl now. Season goals: prove it's a program, not a comet — the exact bet Connelly makes by refusing to project them below the top 10.
deep.prediction: 10-2, a third straight CFP, and no repeat — the October gauntlet (at Ohio State's building flipped: OSU comes to Bloomington Oct. 17, then at Michigan Oct. 24, at Washington Nov. 21) takes one bite, and the title-defense tax takes another. But betting against Cignetti has been the worst trade in college football for two years, and if Hoover cuts the picks, all bets are off. For your listeners: this is the revenge tour's final boss, and the rematch can only happen in the CFP.

Ohio State.ob: Pro-spread under Ryan Day with new OC Arthur Smith (ex-Falcons HC/Steelers OC, hired after Brian Hartline left for the USF job) — expect more 12/13 personnel, play-action, and a real run-game commitment layered onto the WR pipeline.
Ohio State.ot: Julian Sayin returns behind Jeremiah Smith (Tate left for the draft) — the highest-ceiling pass game in the country, now with Smith's NFL wide-zone/keeper structure around it. The 2025 season ended in a Cotton Bowl CFP loss; the offense, not the defense, was the reason.
Ohio State.db: Multiple front under Matt Patricia — disguised coverage shells, NFL-style game-planning, elite pass defense metrics.
Ohio State.dt: Top-5 in EPA allowed and pass efficiency defense in 2025. They rally-tackle and squeeze explosives; pressure comes from scheme more than raw blitzing.
s: Sayin-to-Smith is the best QB-WR axis in the sport
Back-to-back elite defenses with NFL scheme diversity
Two former NFL head coaches now coordinate both sides
w: First-year college OC install — NFL-to-CFB transitions are not automatic
OL play, not skill talent, decides their CFP ceiling
Run-game identity was still optional against elite fronts in 2025

deep.intro: 2025: 12-2 — a 12-0 regular season (including 14-7 over Texas in the opener), then 24 combined points in the CCG loss to Indiana and the Cotton Bowl loss to Miami. SP+: #1 overall, #1 defense, #2 offense, 10.2 avg wins — with the No. 4 schedule. CBS: 11-1, loses the Indy rematch to Oregon, makes the CFP at-large. Vegas: 9.5 total, +185 conference, +600 national favorite.
deep.roster: Julian Sayin returns after a ruthlessly efficient first year (first nationally in success rate, trained-wheels version), and so does the best player in college football: Jeremiah Smith, entering his last dance with 2,558 yards and 29 TDs in two seasons. Sophomore RB Bo Jackson emerged as a yards-after-contact monster; three O-line starters return, including All-Big Ten pair Luke Montgomery and Austin Siereveld. The receiver depth behind Smith is the offensive question — only Jackson and Brandon Inniss were targeted more than five times among returnees — answered with transfers Devin McCuin (UTSA) and Kyle Parker (LSU) plus five-star freshman Chris Henry Jr. The flip side is a defensive exodus: only two of the nine every-game starters return (S Jaylen McClain, CB Jermaine Mathews Jr.), with three defenders drafted in the first 11 picks. Day signed eight defensive transfers — Alabama's Qua Russaw and James Smith up front, Duke's Terry Moore (a Connelly favorite) and FSU's Earl Little Jr. behind — and will lean on a sophomore wave: Zion Grady, Riley Pettijohn, Devin Sanchez, Leroy Roker III. New OC: Arthur Smith, seven years an NFL coordinator/head coach. Matt Patricia returns for Year 2 running the defense. Watch list: Smith (obviously), Jackson, Sanchez, and whichever WR2 emerges.
deep.identity: Offense: last year it was slow-tempo, short-throw efficiency ball — first in success rate, last in urgency, and it died in the two games that mattered (the fourth-quarter punting drive against Miami will live in Columbus infamy). Arthur Smith's history says run-game excellence plus safe horizontal passing — which is either the perfect maturation of Sayin or more of the same disease with an NFL accent. Defense: back-to-back No. 1 in SP+ under Patricia, now facing a genuine transition year. Strengths: the QB-WR1 duo, O-line, recruiting conveyor, and the projection systems' unanimous belief. Weaknesses: tempo/urgency in big games, defensive continuity, WR depth, and a schedule that removes cruise control — at Texas Week 2, at Indiana, at USC, at Iowa, Oregon in November. Season goals: explicitly, the Big Ten title Day hasn't won since 2020, and a January where the offense attacks instead of protects. Connelly's sharpest point: the harder schedule and defensive turnover might force the urgency that's been missing, and that could make them better in December.
deep.prediction: 11-1 with the loss coming in one of the road gauntlet games, a CCG berth, and a CFP run that finally answers the Day question one way or the other. I'd take them over Oregon in Indy — but note that Crawford wouldn't, and the 13-10 CCG loss plus the Miami game means Day enters the year 0-for-his-last-2 in games decided by his own aggression. The stakes for your show: if Ohio State wins it all, the Miami quarterfinal becomes the sport's favorite "that's what woke them up" story.

Notre Dame.ob: Duo/counter downhill run game under Mike Denbrock — 12/13 personnel mixed with CJ Carr's rhythm play-action.
Notre Dame.ot: Even post-Jeremiyah Love, the identity is RB-led: gap-scheme runs, heavy sets, then max-protect shots. Carr showed a live arm as a first-year starter; they'll trust him more in 2026.
Notre Dame.db: Tite-front quarters-match — sound, positionally disciplined, built to erase explosives first.
Notre Dame.dt: Bend-don't-break with elite tackling. They trade some havoc for structure; screens and QB run are the historical soft spots.
s: Carr's year-two leap is the highest-probability QB improvement in the top 10
Freeman's roster has no bottom-quartile position group
w: Independent schedule means zero bad-week amnesty
WR separation vs elite man coverage is still a question
Pass rush relies on scheme more than a dominant edge

Oregon.ob: Lanning's spread — wide zone base with layered RPOs and motion at the conference's highest rate — now under a new play-caller after OC Will Stein left to become Kentucky's head coach.
Oregon.ot: Dante Moore distributes to a rebuilt-but-elite skill room — now with Dylan Raiola arriving from Nebraska as the premium QB2/heir, the Nix-Moore apprenticeship model repeating. They sequence runs into shots better than anyone in the conference.
Oregon.db: 4-2-5 rebuilt on both coordinator chairs: Will Stein (OC) left for the Kentucky job and DC Tosh Lupoi took the Cal job — Lanning's system continuity is the through-line.
Oregon.dt: The scheme (movement fronts, deep DL rotation, explosive-play denial) is Lanning's more than any coordinator's, but two first-year play-callers is real friction — verify both hires before air.
s: Best roster construction in the Big Ten — portal and HS classes both top-5
Special teams and field position consistently elite
w: Have not won the biggest CFP-stage game yet under Lanning
Interior DL vs power run games (see: physical B1G losses)
Stein's exit means a new voice calling it for Dante Moore

deep.intro: 2025: 13-2 (8-1) — both losses to Indiana (regular season and the Peach Bowl semifinal). Now 1-5 against eventual title-game teams over three years and 37-0 against everybody else. SP+: #2, 10.2 avg wins, #1 offense. CBS: 11-1 (lone loss at Ohio State), then beats OSU in the Indy rematch — Big Ten champs. Vegas: 10.5 total (over is popular at +116), +270.
deep.roster: The most important returnee in the sport: QB Dante Moore, a projected top-10 pick who came back after 3,565 yards and 30 TDs, and he brought his whole defensive senior class with him — Matayo Uiagalelei, Bear Alexander, A'Mauri Washington, and Teitum Tuioti all passed on the NFL. Oregon leads the nation with 200 returning starts. The sophomore skill wave is absurd: RBs Jordon Davison and Dierre Hill Jr. (1,323 combined yards at 7.0 a carry, 20 TDs), WRs Dakorien Moore and Jeremiah McClellan, plus a healthy Evan Stewart and transfers Iverson Hooks and Simeon Price. The secondary might have the country's highest upside — sophomores Brandon Finney Jr. and Aaron Flowers plus transfers Koi Perich (poached from Minnesota — an intraconference heist Connelly loves) and Carl Williams IV (Baylor). The two cracks: both coordinators left for head jobs (Stein to Kentucky, Lupoi to Cal), replaced by internal promotions Drew Mehringer (OC) and Chris Hampton (DC); and the O-line is rebuilt for a second straight year around All-American center Iapani Laloulu, with a Yale transfer (Michael Bennett III) and a sophomore (Fox Crader) candidates at tackle. Watch list: Moore (Heisman shortlist), Dakorien Moore, Uiagalelei, Perich.
deep.identity: Offense: No. 1 in projected offensive SP+ — spacing, tempo variation, and the deepest skill room in the country. Defense: a big, ridiculously experienced front six with a ball-hawking back end — Connelly says he'd take Oregon's defensive personnel over almost anyone's. Strengths: continuity, two-deep talent, coaching stability at the top (Lanning is 48-8). Weaknesses: the O-line has been physically overwhelmed in each of the last two CFP exits — that is THE program-defining flaw — plus first-year coordinators in the biggest games. Season goals: unspoken but universal — stop being the best team that can't beat the final boss. Trips to USC (Week 4) and Ohio State (Nov. 7) are the season's proof points; Indiana looms only in Indy.
deep.prediction: 11-1, Big Ten champions (I'll ride with Crawford here — the roster balance argument is real), a top-2 seed, and the "is it finally Oregon's turn" question answered in January rather than November. If the tackles hold up against elite fronts, this is the most complete team in the country. If they don't, it's 37-0-against-everyone-else, part four.

Miami.ob: Shannon Dawson's one-back spread — tempo, quick game to the boundary, vertical seams — behind Cristobal's mauling OL.
Miami.ot: The portal answer arrived: Darian Mensah from Duke — the ACC's leading passer (3,973 yds, 34/6 TD-INT) who won the league title against them. Pair that with a top-10 line-yards OL and this is the ACC's most complete offense on paper.
Miami.db: Corey Hetherman's attack front — high sim-pressure rate, aggressive run fits, man-heavy behind it.
Miami.dt: Big havoc jump in 2025; they trade structure for negative plays. Vertical shots off play-action are the counterpunch opponents live on.
s: Elite OL/DL two-deep — Cristobal recruits the trenches like nobody in the ACC
Mensah is the conference's best returning QB resume
2025 national runner-up — took Indiana to the wire in the title game
w: Aggression = explosives allowed when pressure whiffs
Mensah's first year in a new building — Duke's spacing isn't Dawson's tempo
Expectations now assume CFP; anything less reopens the Cristobal questions

deep.intro: Coach: Mario Cristobal, Year 5 (35-19). Ice cold seat — he's coaching for a natty, not his job.
deep.roster:
Who left: This is a top-heavy exodus. QB Carson Beck is out of eligibility after the title-game run. The defensive front lost its three biggest wreckers — edges Rueben Bain Jr. and Akheem Mesidor plus LB Wesley Bissainthe — and four offensive line starters are gone. WRs Keelan Marion and CJ Daniels graduated, and WR Joshisa Trader left via portal (to NC State — file that for the Wolfpack section). Overall returning production is just 49% (78th nationally), which is the one structural yellow flag on the roster.

Who arrived (portal — No. 4 class nationally per 247Sports, only 12 commits, pure quality-over-quantity): The headliner of the entire sport's offseason: QB Darian Mensah from Duke — 3,973 yards, 34 TD, 6 INT, 66.8% for the defending ACC champs, poached in a late-January portal move so telegraphed (and legally messy — the Duke NIL dispute was settled) that it instantly created the league's most bitter storyline. He brought his No. 1 target with him: WR Cooper Barkate (72-1,106-7 at Duke). The big-play fix was surgical: Cam Vaughn (West Virginia, 15.5 ypc) and Vandrevius Jacobs (South Carolina, 17.1 ypc) join Barkate (15.4). On defense: edge Damon Wilson II (Missouri — 9 sacks, elite 13.9% pressure rate) replaces Bain's juice, S Omar Thornton (BC) adds violence, and OL Jamal Meriweather (Georgia) is the lone line transfer.

Recruits: The crown jewel is five-star OT Jackson Cantwell, the nation's top tackle, who could start as a true freshman. Only ~6 redshirt freshmen on the roster — this team is built to win right now.

Players to watch: Malachi Toney is the answer to everything — 1,324 yards from scrimmage and 11 TDs as a true freshman on screens, jets, and intermediate work, plus elite punt returning, and he's already drawing Heisman dark-horse buzz. RB trio Mark Fletcher Jr. (playoff star), CharMar Brown, and Girard Pringle Jr. all return. The secondary might be the nation's best: six of eight DBs with 200+ snaps are back, all sophomores/juniors — CBs Xavier Lucas and Ethan O'Connor, safety Bryce Fitzgerald. DTs Justin Scott, Ahmad Moten Sr., and Armondo Blount anchor the interior; Marquise Lightfoot is the internal edge answer opposite Wilson.
deep.identity:
Offense: Explosive-hunting spread. Last year's attack was efficient but starved for big plays (fell from 1st to 22nd in offensive SP+); Mensah + three 15-yards-per-catch receivers is the direct fix. Connelly's read: could be more explosive but less efficient, because the O-line is the whole ballgame — two seasoned guards (Matthew McCoy, who may kick to tackle, and Samson Okunlola), then reserves, one transfer, and a true-freshman five-star.

Defense: Surged to 7th in SP+ in 2025 and carried the playoff run. The back end should be elite; the question is whether a rebuilt front six can generate anything close to last year's pass rush. If Wilson and Lightfoot approximate the Bain/Mesidor duo, this is a top-10 unit again.

Goals: Nothing less than the first ACC title in program history, a top-4 seed, and finishing the drive they didn't finish against Indiana. Only two real land mines: at Clemson Oct. 3 and at Notre Dame Nov. 7 (they avoid SMU and Louisville entirely).
deep.prediction: CBS says 12-0 and the No. 1 seed; SP+ says 9.8 wins and the league's only real playoff horse; Vegas says 10.5 and -135. Split the difference: 11-1 or better, ACC championship game, playoff lock barring a Mensah injury. Coach status: untouchable.

Texas Tech.ob: Mack Leftwich's tempo spread — Air-Raid DNA with a real run game, top-25 explosiveness, fastest-scripting offense in the Big 12.
Texas Tech.ot: Behren Morton's exit hands the keys to a portal/blue-chip competition. They push tempo (73.9 plays/g) and hunt shots; success rate (#61) lags the highlight reel — drive-finishing came from defense-gifted fields.
Texas Tech.db: Shiel Wood's 3-3-5 — tite front, pressure from every second-level angle. #1 EPA defense, #1 success rate allowed, #1 havoc in 2025.
Texas Tech.dt: The best defense money could build: bought the entire DL two-deep from the portal and it worked. They win 1st down, then tee off. Question is repeatability after NFL departures (Bailey, Rodriguez).
s: Reigning #1 defense with the resources to re-stock
Tempo + explosiveness stresses any defense's conditioning
Administration fully committed to paying for it
w: New QB in a system that needs one
2025 was aided by a friendly schedule draw — margins shrink
Can the portal-built model survive year-over-year turnover?

deep.intro: 2025: 12-2 (9-1), Big 12 champions, No. 3 in SP+ — beat BYU and Utah three combined times by a 97-24 aggregate, lost only to Arizona State (without their starting QB) and Oregon (shutout, CFP quarterfinal). 2026: McGuire Year 5. SP+: #7, 10.7 avg wins, 62.9% at 11+. Vegas: -105 to repeat, +1600 national.
deep.roster: Start with what's gone: Sorsby (never played a snap — see the saga above), plus maybe the two best defensive players in America (edge David Bailey: 23 TFLs, 14.5 sacks; LB Jacob Rodriguez and his absurd 16.6% tackle rate), four of six top defensive linemen, and the top two receivers. Now what's left and added, which is why nobody's panicking: the QB room is Kirk Francis (Tulsa transfer, 12 career starts, 3,045 yards — likely the Week 1 starter), Will Hammond (the electric sophomore who nearly beat Utah in relief last September, returning from an October ACL and possibly not ready for the opener), plus redshirt freshman Lloyd Jones III and true freshman Stephen Cannon. The backfield is a three-headed monster: Cameron Dickey and J'Koby Williams (1,992 combined yards, 20 TDs) plus Quinten Joyner, the USC transfer who averaged 7.6 a carry in 2024 before missing 2025 hurt. Three O-line starters return, joined by Louisville guard Jordan Church and five-star freshman tackle Felix Ojo. The receiver reload: Kenny Johnson (Pitt — also an elite returner), Malcolm Simmons (Auburn — whose per-catch and per-route numbers beat Cam Coleman's), Donte Lee Jr., Jalen Jones. The defensive reload is a Blanchard portfolio piece — Connelly literally says he can't pretend to worry: tackles Julien Laventure and Jojo Johnson (21 combined TFLs), edges Adam Trick (a Connelly top-10 transfer) and Trey White (25.5 combined TFLs), linebackers Austin Romaine (K-State) and Corey Platt Jr. (Houston), around returnees Ben Roberts and A.J. Holmes Jr. The secondary — Brice Pollock (five INTs, 11.3 QBR allowed), Amier Boyd, Brenden Jordan, John Curry — might be the league's best. Watch list: the QB timeline, Dickey, Trick, Pollock.
deep.identity: Offense: Mack Leftwich's tempo-spread with a bell-cow run game — a touch all-or-nothing last year, now with more proven receivers and less proven quarterbacking. Defense: Shiel Wood's havoc machine, rebuilt with the portal's most disruptive available humans. Strengths: two-deep talent nobody in the league approaches, the No. 2 portal class + No. 19 recruiting class (both league bests), a schedule with no top-90 nonconference opponent and none of the league's next three contenders. Weaknesses: the QB room's combined résumé is one Tulsa season and one great relief outing, and the memory of a playoff shutout when the QB was the ceiling. Also: the political wounds from the Sorsby fight are real, and every league rival is rooting for the collapse. Season goals: repeat, then win a CFP game — the only box left.
deep.prediction: 11-1, back to Arlington on the new Friday night stage, and a top-6 seed. The one loss comes on the road somewhere unglamorous (Houston's Week 3 visit to Lubbock is the first real test — flag it). But the January question is unchanged from last year: can this QB room win a playoff game against an elite defense? McGuire's seat is ice; the dynasty question is the only one that matters.

Georgia.ob: Mike Bobo pro-style — 12 personnel base, condensed formations, play-action keepers for Gunner Stockton's legs.
Georgia.ot: More QB-run element with Stockton than the Bennett/Beck eras. Explosiveness has been the missing gear two straight years; they manufacture chunk plays with tight-end seams rather than WR speed.
Georgia.db: Smart/Schumann multiple 3-4 — pattern-match zone, elite vs the run, linebacker-driven.
Georgia.dt: Still top-shelf on early downs, but 2025 showed uncharacteristic explosive leaks in the secondary. The front seven reloads annually; the back end is the retooling project.
s: Line-of-scrimmage depth on both sides remains SEC-best
Smart's floor: double-digit wins in every full season
Stockton's mobility adds a red-zone dimension
w: WR separation and vertical speed — two years running
Secondary explosives allowed trending the wrong way
Offense can go conservative in exactly the games that decide seasons

deep.intro: 2025: 12-2 (8-1), SEC champions, lost the Sugar Bowl CFP quarterfinal to Ole Miss. SP+: #4, 9.8 avg wins. CBS: 11-1 regular season (lone loss: at South Carolina — a genuinely spicy upset pick), loses SEC CG to Texas, No. 5 seed. Vegas: 9.5 total, +310.
deep.roster: This is the continuity monster: 68% returning production (top-10 nationally), a league-high 169 returning starts, and 13 of the 18 defenders who logged 200+ snaps back. QB Gunner Stockton returns to run what Connelly memorably calls a "blue-chip service academy offense" — extreme efficiency, sideline-to-sideline stress, minimal risk. Backs Nate Frazier and Chauncey Bowens return, with Kentucky bruiser Dante Dowdell added via portal, behind seven linemen with starting experience (four of them sophomores). The portal adds were surgical rather than splashy: Georgia Tech deep threat Isiah Canion (14.6 ypc) to attack the big-play problem, Clemson safety Khalil Barnes for the back end. The defense returns a terrifying sophomore trio — DTs Elijah Griffin and Joseph Jonah-Ajonye plus CB Ellis Robinson IV — and Connelly calls the tackle rotation the deepest in the country. Watch list: Griffin (potential No. 1-pick trajectory), Robinson, London Humphreys (15.3 ypc as the returning field-stretcher).
deep.identity: Offense: Mike Bobo conservatism — quick game, huge success rate, allergic to explosives. That's the entire Georgia debate in one sentence: they've finished sixth in SP+ two straight years, they've lost two straight playoff games, and the culprit both times is an offense that doesn't generate big plays even though the defense doesn't allow any. Defense: no unit in the conference gets more benefit of the doubt — eight games allowing 14 or fewer points last year with a young lineup that's now experienced. Strengths: DT depth, offensive efficiency, special teams (3rd in SP+), the schedule (avoids Texas, A&M, and LSU in the regular season and is projected favorite in all 12 games). Weakness: explosiveness, full stop. Season goals: a fifth straight trip to Atlanta is close to assumed; the real goal is winning three playoff games in December/January, which requires an offense that can score fast when trailing.
deep.prediction: The floor is the highest in America: 10-2 is the realistic worst case. I'd take the CBS shape — 11-1, SEC title game — but I'd flip the CG result and take Georgia over Texas, because Kirby is 3-0 against Sark's Longhorns including the 2024 title game. Call it 11-1, SEC champs, No. 2/3 seed, and a semifinal-or-better run. The Nov. 7 trip to Oxford is the revenge game of the SEC season (Golding is 1-0 lifetime against Kirby, from the Sugar Bowl). Hot seat: nonexistent — but note the streak on the line: Kirby hasn't won fewer than 10 in a full season since 2016.

Utah.ob: Morgan Scalley finally takes over (HC-in-waiting since 2024) after Whittingham's exit to Michigan — Jason Beck's QB-run spread around Devon Dampier should carry over intact.
Utah.ot: Dampier returning keeps the identity: zone-read, counter bash, designed keepers. Scalley ran the defense for a decade, so offensive continuity is the plan, not a hope — but confirm Beck stayed before air.
Utah.db: Morgan Scalley's 4-2-5 — structurally disciplined, safeties downhill, almost never misaligned.
Utah.dt: Classic Utah: top-20 success-rate defense, low havoc variance, tackle everything. They'll trade completions for time-of-possession and red-zone stops.
s: Scalley is the most prepared first-year HC in the country
Dampier is the Big 12's most valuable returning QB

deep.intro: 2025: 11-2, 8th in SP+ — Whittingham's farewell masterpiece, ten wins by double digits. 2026 coach: Morgan Scalley, first year — the fourth Utah head coach since 1989, promoted after two decades as the defensive architect. SP+: #24, 8.4 avg wins. Vegas: 8.5, sub-+1000.
deep.roster: The two keepers are enormous: QB Devon Dampier (the dual-threat engine of a No. 6 offense that led the nation in red-zone TD rate) and 900-yard RB Wayshawn Parker. But they're the ONLY offensive returnees with more than five starts, OC Jason Beck followed Whittingham to Michigan, and all five line starters are gone. Scalley's fixes: Utah State OC Kevin McGiven (another Bronco Mendenhall-tree dual-threat schemer) with his top receiver Braden Pegan in tow, Montana State OL transfer Cedric Jefferson, and a senior-heavy backup pipeline. The defense keeps stars (LB Johnathan Hall, nickel Jackson Bennee) but lost its top four linemen and three of four DBs; longtime LB coach Colton Swan takes the coordinator chair, transfers Elijah Reed and James Chenault patch the corners, and the veteran leadership layer (Holtzclaw, Reynolds, Ritchie) carries the culture bet. Watch list: Dampier, Hall, the entire offensive line.
deep.identity: Offense: dual-threat leverage ball under a new-but-familiar schemer. Defense: Scalley's own top-20 machine, now delegated. Strengths: QB, culture continuity, a friendly schedule (misses Tech, hosts BYU). Weaknesses: five new O-linemen, a rebuilt defensive front and secondary, and the succession question — Connelly notes promoted-coordinator defenses don't always stay elite. Season goals: keep the machine humming through the transition, win the Holy War at home Nov. 7, survive the brutal back-to-back at Arizona (Nov. 14) and at TCU (Nov. 21).
deep.prediction: 9-3 — the November three-week gauntlet (BYU, at Arizona, at TCU) claims one game a fresh line can't survive, and Utah plays spoiler-kingmaker rather than champion. But the floor here is remarkably high for a Year 1 coach; three coaches in 36 years is the whole story of why.

Ole Miss.ob: Pete Golding's first year as HC after Kiffin's LSU exit — but the spread-tempo offensive core persists, now built around a returning star.
Ole Miss.ot: Trinidad Chambliss is BACK for 2026 after winning his NCAA eligibility appeal — the reigning SEC Newcomer of the Year who quarterbacked a 13-win, first-ever CFP season (Sugar Bowl MVP in the win over Georgia; season ended in the Fiesta vs Miami). Ex-Auburn five-star Deuce Knight waits behind him.
Ole Miss.db: Golding's own 4-2-5 system stays — multiple fronts, sim pressures, transfer-heavy DL.
Ole Miss.dt: The identity Golding built as DC: disruptive interior line, aggressive early-down run fits, press man in must-pass. He kept the defensive spine through the transition.
s: Chambliss chose Oxford over following Kiffin — the rarest kind of continuity win
Program-record 13-win CFP season as the springboard
QB room is two-deep with high-end talent (Chambliss + Knight)
w: First-year head coach following the program's identity engine
Lost OC-level play-calling brains to Baton Rouge
The league now has a full offseason of Chambliss film

deep.intro: 2025: 13-2 (7-1) — the chaos team of the decade: Kiffin leaves pre-CFP, Golding goes 2-1 in the playoff with wins over Tulane and Georgia before the one-play-short Fiesta loss to Miami. 2026 coach: Pete Golding, first full year. SP+: #15, 7.2 avg wins (No. 7 schedule). CBS: 9-3, No. 11 seed — beating LSU and Georgia along the way. Vegas: 7.5 (Talty: over, -150 — his second-favorite over on the board), +950.
deep.roster: The miracle of the offseason: Golding kept the core despite Kiffin's open poaching. QB Trinidad Chambliss is back — via lawsuit and injunction after the NCAA denied his extra year — along with 300-carry RB Kewan Lacy, DT Will Echoles, and LB Suntarine Perkins. All four finished 2025 as top-20-caliber players. What left mostly went to Baton Rouge (Umanmielen, Dottery, Watkins) or Columbia, Missouri (Austin Simmons — more on him below). The reload: new OC John David Baker (East Carolina, up-tempo), four transfer edges led by Nevada's Jonathan Maldonado, a comically large interior with 355-lb Michai Boireau (Florida) and 325-lb Jehiem Oatis (Colorado/Bama), LBs Keaton Thomas (Baylor) and Luke Ferrelli (Cal), and — the pressure point — a secondary returning only three of nine, patched with Georgia's Joenel Aguero and FSU's Edwin Joseph. RB relief comes from Makhi Frazier (Michigan State) and 1,676-yard FCS rusher Joshua Dye; tackles Carius Curne (LSU) and Tommy Kinsler IV (Miami — a Canes-connection note for your show) push the line. Watch list: Chambliss (the sport's best chaos agent — his escape against Georgia was the play of the postseason), Lacy, Maldonado, Aguero.
deep.identity: Offense: tempo, QB-run leverage, and Chambliss's pressure-escape wizardry — he was statistically the best in the country at turning dead plays into first downs. Defense: Golding's own unit slipped to 20th last year; the bet is that a rebuilt pass rush and an immovable interior restore the 2024 form. Strengths: QB, run game, special teams (No. 1 in SP+ — a genuinely elite hidden edge), house-money culture. Weaknesses: secondary turnover, O-line negative plays, and the small-sample question — Golding has coached three games, and Dabo's tampering accusation plus the NCAA probe hang over the program. Season goals: prove the playoff run was a program, not a moment — and beat Kiffin on Sept. 19 in what might be the most emotionally charged game of the 2026 season anywhere.
deep.prediction: Connelly's framing is right: top-five ceiling, top-20 floor. I'll take 9-3 and a second straight CFP bid, with the Kiffin game as a win — Oxford that night will be a hostile-environment record attempt. The Nov. 7 Georgia rematch decides seeding. Golding has no seat to speak of; he has the opposite problem — expectations inflated by a three-game sample, an NCAA cloud, and the sport's most vindictive ex living one state over.

Alabama.ob: Ryan Grubb's dropback pass game — five-out spacing, chunk throws to the intermediate middle, RB screens as run-game extension.
Alabama.ot: Ty Simpson's draft departure hands it to Keelon Russell (five-star, year two). Pass-first identity is set; the run game (#102 rush EPA feel in stretches) and short-yardage remain the friction points.
Alabama.db: Kane Wommack's 4-2-5 'swarm' — light boxes, rally speed, disguised pressure.
Alabama.dt: Elite in coverage, occasionally soft vs downhill gap runs. Special teams (SP+ ST -1.30, #103) actively lost them field position all season — a fixable, glaring leak.
s: Russell is the most talented QB in the building since Bryce Young
WR room reloads with five-stars annually
Wommack's coverage disguise ranks with anyone's
w: Special teams was a bottom-35 unit — inexcusable at this level
First-year starter at QB in Tuscaloosa expectations

deep.intro: 2025: 11-4 (7-2) — beat Georgia in Tuscaloosa, lost the SEC CG rematch 28-7, won a CFP first-round game, lost the quarterfinal. Also lost the opener to Florida State and collapsed late. SP+: #11, 8.0 avg wins. CBS: 9-3, first team out of the CFP (losses: Georgia, A&M, at LSU). Vegas: 8.5 (Talty: over), +850.
deep.roster: This is the scariest continuity profile of any blue blood: 48% returning production (90th nationally), 35% on offense — dead last in the conference tier. Gone: QB Ty Simpson (NFL), leading rusher Jam Miller, three of Simpson's top four targets, and six of the top seven offensive linemen. DeBoer deliberately skipped the veteran-QB portal market and is handing the offense to either redshirt freshman Keelon Russell — the No. 2 overall prospect in the 2025 class, nearly flawless in a tiny 2025 sample — or longtime DeBoer backup Austin Mack. The only notable skill transfer, NC State WR Noah Rogers, is already hurt (spring injury, out to start the season). The defense is the inverse: Kane Wommack returns maybe the best secondary in America — CBs Zabien Brown and Dijon Lee Jr., nickel Red Morgan, safeties Bray Hubbard and Keon Sabb — plus potential All-American edge Yhonzae Pierre. But four of the top five interior D-linemen are gone, patched with transfers Devan Thompkins (USC), Kedrick Bingley-Jones (Miss State), and Terrance Green (Oregon). Watch list: Russell (the whole season), Pierre, sophomore skill wave (Lotzeir Brooks, Rico Scott, Derek Meadows, Kaleb Edwards), freshman RB Ezavier Crowell.
deep.identity: Offense: DeBoer's rhythm-and-spacing passing game — except last year it finished 36th in offensive SP+, the run game was dreadful, and the No. 1 receiver had the country's worst high-volume drop rate (13.0%). Now it's the same scheme with a teenager at the wheel and a rebuilt line. Defense: top-five SP+ projection, elite coverage, aggressive structure. Strengths: secondary, edge rush, the sheer recruiting talent stockpile, a soft launch (no projected top-20 opponent until Week 6 — which is Georgia, Oct. 10). Weaknesses: total offensive inexperience, interior lines both ways, special teams (107th last year, projected 109th — a real hidden-points leak), and early landmines: Kentucky Week 2, an FSU revenge game Week 3 (they lost that opener last year), South Carolina Week 4. Season goals: DeBoer has lost as many games in two years as Saban lost in his final five. Year 3 is the referendum.
deep.prediction: Prediction & the seat. The honest range: if Russell is the generational player his recruiting ranking says, this is a 10-2 playoff team and the QB story of the year. If he's a normal redshirt freshman behind a new line, 8-4 with an ugly October is absolutely live, and CBS's "first team out" pick becomes the kind description. I'll take 9-3, narrowly out of the field, and a full-blown Tuscaloosa crisis of faith by Thanksgiving. How the seat got warm: eight losses in two years at a program that measured itself in undefeated regular seasons; a late-2025 fade; an offense that regressed; and the optics of skipping the QB portal market while rivals spent. How he escapes:** Russell hits, Bama beats Georgia or LSU (or both), 10+ wins with a playoff appearance. Anything less and the DeBoer question stops being whispered. The nightmare scenario nobody in Alabama says out loud: 8-4 while Kiffin wins big at LSU with the roster-building model Bama declined to run.

Texas.ob: Sarkisian pro-style — wide zone marriage with play-action dagger/flood, 12 personnel sprinkled, Arch Manning at the wheel.
Texas.ot: 2025 underdelivered on the preseason #1 hype: OL regression and drops, not Manning, were the story. Expect more designed Manning movement and a rebuilt interior line in 2026.
Texas.db: Will Muschamp takes over the defense after Pete Kwiatkowski's exit — the riskiest coordinator change in the league given the unit outranked the offense two straight years.
Texas.dt: The back end returns real depth (McDonald, Littleton, portal CB Bo Mascoe) and Colin Simmons is an elite edge — but Muschamp's last top-10 defense was 2014. Scheme continuity is the open question under the hood of a title-or-bust roster.
s: Manning's arm talent + second full year as starter
PK's secondary erases one side of the field
Roster depth built for a 16-game season
w: OL interior was the 2025 disappointment — retooled, unproven
Run-game identity wobbles when zone gets stuffed early
Pressure to deliver now is at maximum

deep.intro: 2025: 10-3 (6-2), preseason No. 1, missed the CFP, throttled Michigan in the Citrus Bowl. SP+: #6, 8.6 avg wins — with the No. 1 schedule in America. CBS: 12-1, SEC champs, No. 2 seed (losses: Ohio State opener only... then beats Georgia in Atlanta). Vegas: 9.5 total (Talty likes the over at +110), +300 — the SEC favorite. Arch Manning is the Heisman favorite.
deep.roster: Arch is back for what's likely his final season, and Sarkisian built the most talked-about portal class in the sport around him — 15 players, almost all of them ranked at the top of their position: Cam Coleman (No. 1 portal WR, from Auburn), Hollywood Smothers (No. 2 RB, from NC State — 4.4 yards after contact per carry, a comical number), Raleek Brown (No. 5 RB, Arizona State), Melvin Siani (No. 3 OT), Rasheem Biles (No. 3 LB, from Pitt — Connelly's favorite transfer in the entire class: 15.8% tackle rate, 15 TFLs, 4.5 sacks, 2 INTs), Bo Mascoe (No. 7 CB, Rutgers), Ian Geffrard (No. 9 DL). Returning: WRs Ryan Wingo and Emmett Mosley V, star edge Colin Simmons (12 sacks), S Jelani McDonald, nickel Graceson Littleton, and 130 returning starts (68% returning production, 6th nationally). Key losses: LBs Anthony Hill Jr. and Liona Lefau, DC Pete Kwiatkowski — replaced, eyebrow-raisingly, by Will Muschamp, whose last top-10 defense was 2014. Watch list: Coleman (highest ceiling in the portal, 7.9% drop rate is the flaw), Simmons, sophomore CB Kade Phillips, freshman WR room (Kaliq Lockett, Jermaine Bishop Jr.).
deep.identity: Offense: Sark's pro-style spread finally has the pieces again after sliding from 6th (2023) to 30th in offensive SP+ — Connelly says there are no excuses left for anything outside the top 10. Arch's lone remaining flaw is accuracy throwing on the run; everything else clicked in November. Defense: ranked ahead of the offense two straight years, which makes firing Kwiatkowski for Muschamp the single riskiest coordinator hire in the league. Strengths: skill-position matchup advantages on every snap, edge rush, secondary depth, the Arch factor. Weaknesses: overall depth (Sark churned so much roster that a couple of injuries bite hard), the Muschamp gamble, and a schedule from hell — eight projected top-20 opponents, with the last three (at LSU Nov. 14, at A&M Nov. 27) on the road. Season goals: title or bust, explicitly. CBS calls it exactly that, on one of the nation's most expensive rosters.
deep.prediction: The talent says 12-1; the schedule says 9-3 wouldn't shock anyone — that spread is the widest in the conference. My read: 10-2, in Atlanta, in the CFP either way — and the Ohio State opener is nearly a free roll (a loss there costs almost nothing in January given the rest of the résumé). Sark isn't on any hot seat, but the subtext is real: Texas has NEVER won the SEC, Arch likely leaves after this year, and a second straight miss with this roster would change the temperature in Austin overnight. Heisman note for your picks segment: Arch at short odds is bad value; Coleman or Stockton late-round flyers are the play.

Texas A&M.ob: Collin Klein's spread — Marcel Reed's legs as a base constraint, quick RPO game, shot plays off sprint-out movement.
Texas A&M.ot: Reed returning after the 2025 CFP run makes this a top-10 offense on paper: designed QB run in the red zone, improving pocket patience. WR depth thinned by the draft.
Texas A&M.db: Mike Elko's multiple front — heavy nickel, twist-game interior pressure, coverage rotation late in the down.
Texas A&M.dt: Elko's fingerprints: top-25 success rate allowed, disciplined vs RPOs. Edge rush production must be re-sourced after draft losses.
s: Reed is the SEC's best returning dual-threat resume
Elko game-days: consistently steal one possession a game
Trench classes stacked three years deep
w: Receiver room needs a portal hit to stay explosive
Program still hasn't stacked two elite years in a row

deep.intro: 2025: 11-2 (7-1) — unbeaten until Texas ruined Thanksgiving, then a CFP first-round exit. SP+: #9, 8.4 avg wins. CBS: 8-4 (losses at LSU, at Oklahoma, Texas... and Alabama flips to a road win). Vegas: 8.5 (Talty: over), +850.
deep.roster: The skill talent that carried an 11-win team is nearly all back: QB Marcel Reed, RB Rueben Owens II, WRs Mario Craver and Ashton Bethel-Roman, plus defensive anchors T.J. Searcy, Dezz Ricks, Marcus Ratcliffe, and Dalton Brooks. LB Daymion Sanford returns but is expected to miss time with injury. What's gone is enormous in a different way: 13 NFL Combine invites, OC Collin Klein, and — this is the story — six of the top seven offensive linemen and five of seven on the defensive line. Elko rebuilt both trenches almost entirely through the portal: four projected transfer OL starters (42 combined SEC starts last year, so at least they're battle-tested), plus five D-line transfers headlined by SDSU's Ryan Henderson and Northwestern's Anto Saka (12.5% pressure rate). Tulsa tackling machine Ray Coney arrives at LB. Two new coordinators: Holmon Wiggins (offense), Lyle Hemphill (defense). Watch list: Craver, Owens, Saka, and freshman-laden depth (21 redshirt freshmen — a modern roster-building flex).
deep.identity: Offense: Reed's dual-threat rhythm game — he was one of only three SEC QBs with 3,000 passing and 550 rushing yards (Chambliss and Pavia the others). The problem: seventh in the league in QBR because of pure mistakes — 12 INTs, four fumbles, a 15th-of-16 catchable-ball rate, and a nightmarish stat line in the two losses (9.3 yards per completion, 0 TD, 4 INT). Defense: Elko's scheme with elite back-seven talent and a total mystery up front. Strengths: returning skill talent, secondary, Elko/GM Derek Miller's roster-building track record, elite 2027 recruiting momentum (A&M currently holds the No. 1 class). Weaknesses: portal-rebuilt lines (Connelly's warning: rebuilding a line through the portal is how otherwise-good teams die), Reed's turnover glitches, six projected top-20 opponents with four on the road. Season goals: prove 2025 wasn't schedule-flavored — CBS notes how weak last year's slate was — and win the games that stamp a contender: LSU, OU, Texas.
deep.prediction: This is the widest gap on the board: SP+ top-10 team, CBS 8-4 team. Split it: 9-3 with the season defined by Sept. 26 in Baton Rouge and Black Friday vs. Texas. If the O-line transfers gel by October, the CFP is live; if Reed throws multi-pick games again, 8-4 and a January of what-ifs. No heat whatsoever on Elko — he's got the juice, the GM structure, and the recruiting board.

USC.ob: Lincoln Riley's Air Raid tree — spacing and leverage masterclass, best screen game in the country, tempo when wounded.
USC.ot: Riley's system produces top-10 pass efficiency regardless of the trigger man; 2026 QB competition (post-Maiava room) decides the ceiling. Run game is opportunistic draw/counter, not identity.
USC.db: D'Anton Lynn's 4-2-5 — simulated pressure, split-safety shells, major 2025 improvement in structure.
USC.dt: From liability to league-average-plus in one Lynn year: fewer busts, better tackling. Still lean up front against B1G power run games.
s: Riley's pass-game design remains a per-play cheat code
Lynn defense trending up with his own recruits
w: Interior DL vs Michigan/Ohio State-style power remains the litmus test
QB transition year in a QB-dependent system
Cold-weather November road identity still unproven

deep.intro: 2025: 9-4 (7-2), 16th in SP+ — the offense (9th) carried a 36th-ranked defense. SP+: #13, 7.8 avg wins, No. 11 schedule. Vegas: +1500.
deep.roster: QB Jayden Maiava returns off a 3,711-yard, 24-TD season with the best QBR of any returning Big Ten starter — virtually unsackable, a perfectly timed scrambler, a point guard. His problem: seven of the top eight receivers are gone. Sophomore Tanook Hines is the star-in-waiting, NC State transfer Terrell Anderson (16.1 per catch, 73.6% catch rate) is the safety valve, and a wave of blue-chip freshmen (Boobie Feaster, Trent Mosley, Mark Bowman) fills in. The backfield is a strength — King Miller (4.2 yards after contact as a walk-on freshman) and Waymond Jordan, plus three returning line starters. The story is the defense: coordinator D'Anton Lynn left for Penn State, and Lincoln Riley replaced him with 66-year-old Gary Patterson, whose last top-20 unit was 2014. Nine of 16 rotation defenders return, most of them former blue-chip underclassmen — Kameryn Crawford, Jahkeem Stewart, Jadyn Walker, Marcelles Williams — plus veteran transfers (Zuriah Fisher, Alex VanSumeren, Deven Bryant) and mega-freshmen (Luke Wafle, Tomuhini Topui). Watch list: Maiava, Hines, Stewart.
deep.identity: Offense: Riley's spacing game with a genuinely elite QB — top-6 projected again. Defense: Patterson's 4-2-5 was the most influential scheme of the 2000s; the question is whether the innovator still has an edge now that everyone runs his ideas. Strengths: QB, backfield, recruiting surge, and finally some roster continuity (65% returning production). Weaknesses: receiver proof, the Patterson bet, and a schedule with five projected top-40 league opponents — including Oregon AND Washington at home back-to-back before the Oct. 10 trip to Penn State. Season goals: the CFP, full stop — Riley's fifth year, most balanced roster, no more excuses.
deep.prediction: 9-3 and the bubble's loudest argument. If Patterson's unit is merely top-30, USC wins ten and gets in; Connelly calls them the conference's most fascinating team for exactly that reason. Riley's seat isn't hot, but a fourth straight season outside the CFP with this roster would start the countdown clock in earnest.

Vanderbilt.ob: Tim Beck's option-flavored spread — but the Diego Pavia era is over, and the offense was built bespoke around his chaos.
Vanderbilt.ot: Two years of program-best offense came from QB-run keepers, fourth-down aggression, and Pavia's off-script genius. The 2026 offense must be re-founded: expect the same run-first, possession-length identity with a more conventional passer.
Vanderbilt.db: Clark Lea's 3-3-5 multiple — his own defensive rebuild caught up in 2025: opportunistic, turnover-hunting.
Vanderbilt.dt: They won 2025 games with third-down disguise and red-zone stinginess rather than raw talent. Regression risk is real as the league re-calibrates to them.
s: Lea has institutionalized an identity Vandy never had
Fourth-down/analytics edge is the sharpest in the SEC
Two straight bowl seasons of belief and retention
w: Replacing Pavia isn't a position change, it's an identity change
Talent gap vs the SEC's top half hasn't closed

deep.intro: 2025: 10-3 (6-2), 11th in SP+, roughly a field goal from the CFP — the greatest season in program history. SP+: #31, 6.5 avg wins — the biggest projected drop in the league (last in SEC returning production). CBS: 6-6 (Talty: under at -170, his most confident lean). Vegas: 6.5, +8000.
deep.roster: Diego Pavia is gone, and with him three of the top four receivers, four starting O-linemen, and eight of eleven front-six regulars. Clark Lea's replacement plan is audacious: five-star true freshman Jared Curtis — the No. 12 overall recruit in the 2026 class, the highest-rated signee in Vandy history — stepping straight into the SEC. The cushions: backs Sedrick Alexander and Makhilyn Young (a combined 6.5 ypc, 17 TDs), portal pass-catchers Ja'Cory Thomas, Cole Adams, and TE Jayvontay Conner, plus veteran transfer linemen Lyndon Cooper (Pitt) and Beau Johnson (NDSU). Defense: the secondary is solid (Martel Hight, Jordan Matthews, CJ Heard, Clemson transfer Ricardo Jones), but the front six is nearly all transfers — Edwin Kolenge (BC), Brian Allen (Iowa), CJ Jackson (LSU), Talan Carter, Michael Diatta. Watch list: Curtis (every snap is a referendum on modern recruiting), Alexander, Hight.
deep.identity: Offense: Tim Beck's explosive, QB-friendly system — 5th in offensive SP+ last year — now asked to protect a freshman instead of weaponizing a sixth-year folk hero. Defense: bend-don't-break with a rebuilt run front, the clear vulnerability. Strengths: special teams (7th), RB room, program-belief compounding. Weaknesses: everything Pavia-shaped, run defense, SEC schedule gravity. Season goals: a bowl in a rebuild year would confirm this is a program now, not a comet.
deep.prediction: 6-6 and a bowl, with Curtis flashing enough to make 2027 the real bet. Lea's seat: nonexistent — 2025 bought him a decade of goodwill, though the league piece dryly notes his ranking rests heavily on one departed quarterback.

Oklahoma.ob: Ben Arbuckle's Air-Raid-with-legs — John Mateer's dual-threat volume, empty formations with QB-draw constraint.
Oklahoma.ot: Mateer confirmed his return for a senior year ('I need to be better' — his words). 2025 was uneven: 62% completion, 14/11 TD-INT through a broken throwing hand he played through after 17 days. Healthy hand + year two in-system is the bull case; the designed-run gravity (8 rushing TDs) never left.
Oklahoma.db: Venables' 4-2-5 — cheat-the-count sim pressures, elite 2025 (top-5 EPA allowed), speed everywhere.
Oklahoma.dt: The best Venables defense yet: havoc without busts. Replacing interior anchors is the annual tax, but the scheme is now fully installed.
s: Mateer returning removes the biggest variable — chemistry with Arbuckle is year three
Venables defense hit elite in year four as promised
The Sooners finally look built for SEC line play
w: Mateer must cut the interceptions (11 in 2025) to justify the billing
WR explosiveness thin past the top two
OL depth one injury from trouble

deep.intro: 2025: 10-3 (6-2), made the CFP on the SEC's best defense, lost a home first-round game. SP+: #12, 7.3 avg wins — with the No. 3 schedule nationally. CBS: 8-4 (losses: at Michigan, at Georgia, Texas). Vegas: 7.5 (Talty: over — "too talented to win less than eight"), +1100.
deep.roster: QB John Mateer is back for Year 2 in the system after an injury-marred 2025 — he spent the offseason rebuilding his throwing motion after the thumb injury. The defense that ranked 4th in SP+ returns 11 of 20 rotation players: edge Taylor Wein, DT David Stone, LBs Kip Lewis and Owen Heinecke (the latter via court injunction — Oklahoma's own eligibility lawsuit win), CBs Courtland Guillory and Eli Bowen, S Peyton Bowen. Four of six D-linemen depart, but Venables has a 10-12 deep pool of blue-chip linemen to reload from. The offensive fix-it list is all portal: RB Lloyd Avant (Colorado State), five OL transfers including Arkansas starter E'Marion Harris, and a completely rebuilt WR room around returning security blanket Isaiah Sategna III — Trell Harris (Virginia), Parker Livingstone (Texas), TEs Hayden Hansen (Florida) and Rocky Beers (CSU). Watch list: Mateer's health (his style invites hits), Sategna, Stone, sophomore RB development.
deep.identity: Offense: Ben Arbuckle's Air Raid derivative, still a work in progress — the 2025 run game ranked 124th in yards per carry, which is nearly impossible with this talent level. Defense: Venables took over play-calling in 2025 and turned it into the SEC's best unit — that's the identity, full stop. Strengths: defensive back seven, Mateer's dual-threat ceiling, Venables' scheme. Weaknesses: run game, receiver cohesion, and the honest truth about 2025 — Connelly notes OU essentially made the playoff on two defensive touchdowns and rarely looked like a top-8 team otherwise. Schedule is brutal: three projected top-15 opponents in the first five games, four straight top-20 opponents to close. Season goals: prove the CFP trip was a floor, not a fluke — and beat Texas for once (Venables is 1-3 in the Red River game).
deep.prediction: Prediction & the seat. 8-4 feels almost predetermined by the schedule, and the regression narrative writes itself if the offense stalls again. But if Mateer stays healthy behind a functional line, this defense travels anywhere — 9-3 with wins over Ole Miss and A&M puts them right back in the bracket. The seat:** technically cooled — a 10-win CFP season buys real credit — but the pattern is bad-good-bad-good with zero great years and zero playoff wins, and USA TODAY's hot seat piece pointedly declined to call him safe beyond "for now." Another 6-win valley and it's over; another 10-win year and he's finally built something.

Penn State.ob: Matt Campbell era begins (8-year deal from Iowa State, replacing the fired James Franklin) — expect his identity: multi-TE pro-style run game, play-action, culture-first program building.
Penn State.ot: Campbell turned Iowa State from 3-9 into a perennial bowl program with less talent than he inherits here. The roster skews power-spread with a top-15 OL two-deep and deep RB room; run-first ball control while the new staff installs, opening vs Marshall Sept 5.
Penn State.db: The defensive front is the program's export product — edge/DT depth stays elite through the transition. Note: 2025 DC Jim Knowles left for Tennessee, so the back end gets a new voice too.
Penn State.dt: 2025 was top-10 in success rate allowed before the season unraveled. Campbell's Iowa State defenses were famously structured (the 3-3-5 revolution started there) — expect scheme discipline over blitz volume.
s: Campbell is a proven program-builder with a top-10 roster for the first time
Trench talent on both sides survives regime change
w: QB position is a genuine unknown in year one
Coordinator turnover on both sides in one offseason
Campbell has never coached a season under this level of expectation

deep.intro: 2025: 7-6 (3-6) — Franklin fired mid-season after three straight one-score losses; the roster still finished 15th in SP+. 2026 coach: Matt Campbell, first year — the Iowa State program-builder, arriving with roughly 24 former Cyclones including 11-13 projected starters. SP+: #17, 9.1 avg wins... on the No. 59 schedule. Vegas: 9.5 total, +2000.
deep.roster: The most extreme turnover experiment among contenders: only seven of 35 players with 200+ snaps return, three of them starters (plus returning-from-injury star LB Tony Rojas). The offense is essentially Iowa State East — QB Rocco Becht, RB Carson Hansen, WRs Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen, TE Benjamin Brahmer, two Cyclone guards — plus Ohio State RB transfer James Peoples and returning TE Andrew Rappleyea and G Anthony Donkoh. The defense is stronger: Rojas, nickel Zion Tracy, and CB Audavion Collins lead the holdovers; Campbell's Cyclone imports (LBs Kooper Ebel and Caleb Bacon, CB Jeremiah Cooper, safeties Marcus Neal Jr. — a Connelly top-10 transfer — and Jamison Patton) merge with four 319-pound-plus transfer tackles; and new DC D'Anton Lynn (from USC) inherits a better culture than he left. Watch list: Becht, Neal, Rojas.
deep.identity: Offense: Campbell ball — physical, patient, low-variance... and drawn from an ISU offense that ranked just 63rd. Defense: Lynn's multiple structure with real talent; this should be top-25 quickly. Strengths: coaching floor, special teams (6th projected), and a schedule gift — only three projected top-40 Big Ten opponents, no Indiana, no Ohio State, no Oregon. Weaknesses: the whole premise — Connelly's warning is that mass transfers usually flow FROM better teams, not from a 33rd-ranked one to a program that hasn't ranked that low since 2015. And the standard: Franklin averaged top-11 finishes; "Iowa State's best" was one top-20 SP+ team in a decade. Season goals: 9-plus wins, a CFP bubble seat, and a culture Campbell can actually recruit to.
deep.prediction: 9-3, right on the CBS number, with the USC visit on Oct. 10 as the season's fork: win it and PSU is a legitimate dark horse (SP+ gives them 13.6% at 11+ wins, fourth in the league); lose it and the schedule-inflation critics take a bow. The strangest range of outcomes of any top-25 team — borderline top-15 and outside the top 40 are both live, per Connelly himself.

BYU.ob: Aaron Roderick's spread — Bear Bachmeier's true-freshman 2025 revelation, QB run married to deep play-action.
BYU.ot: Bachmeier returning for year two is the Big 12's quiet superpower: they punished single-high all season. Physical, patient, and better on 3rd-and-4-to-7 than anyone in the league.
BYU.db: Jay Hill's 3-3-5 — boundary pressure looks, disciplined eyes, turnover manufacturing.
BYU.dt: Top-20 havoc without gambling: Hill's blitzes hit home because the back end holds. Size vs SEC-style OLs is the only structural question.
s: Bachmeier is the best returning QB story in the Big 12
Hill's defense creates 2+ takeaways a game feel
Roster continuity in the portal era — nobody retains like BYU
w: Skill-position ceiling is modest by top-10 standards
Front sizes get tested by the league's biggest OLs
Thin margin for error with a top-heavy schedule

deep.intro: 2025: 12-2 (8-2), 23-4 over two years, CCG runner-up — both losses to Tech by a combined 49 points. SP+: #18, 8.7 avg wins. Vegas: 8.5 total (CBS projected ~10-2), sub-+1000 title odds.
deep.roster: Bear Bachmeier returns after a true-freshman season Connelly calls deliciously high-level — the safest QB bet in the conference. LJ Martin (1,300 yards) returns behind four veteran linemen including all-conference center Bruce Mitchell, plus Washington tackle transfer Paki Finau. The flags: the top three pass targets are gone (Martin and USC TE transfer Walker Lyons are the safety valves; Jojo Phillips and Oregon transfer Kyler Kasper are big-bodied potential, nothing more yet), and DC Jay Hill left for Michigan — longtime assistant Kelly Poppinga inherits ten returning rotation defenders, headlined by LB pair Isaiah Glasker and Siale Esera, corners Evan Johnson and Therrian Alexander III, and 6-4 safety Faletau Satuala. Young edges Nusi Taumoepeau and Tausili Akana carry the pressure-rate promise of a sack breakout. Watch list: Bachmeier, Glasker, the WR mystery.
deep.identity: Offense: efficient, physical, QB-run-flavored — potentially BYU's best since the legendary 2020 unit if receivers emerge. Defense: experienced everywhere with a new voice in the booth. Strengths: continuity (177 returning starts, tops in the league), QB, culture, home-field. Weaknesses: pass-catching proof, pass rush conversion, the Hill departure. Season goals: get over the Tech hump they never touched — and win the two biggest home games in program memory: Arizona Sept. 12 and NOTRE DAME in mid-October.
deep.prediction: 10-2 and the league's second CFP bid — with the Notre Dame game as a season-defining at-large résumé bomb either way. Connelly's running joke that he's always wrong about BYU is doing a lot of work here; the fundamentals say top-15, and this time there's no Tech on the schedule until Arlington. Sitake's seat: nonexistent; he's built the second-best program in the league.

Iowa.ob: Tim Lester's outside zone — 12/21 personnel, the slowest pace in the sample (60.8 plays/g), play-action boots off it.
Iowa.ot: Gronowski's graduation reopens the eternal QB question. Elite line yards (#15) and points/opportunity (#20) say the infrastructure works; they shorten every game and bet on special teams.
Iowa.db: Phil Parker's quarters — two-high shells, drop-eight in must-pass, tackling machine.
Iowa.dt: #8 in explosiveness allowed: nothing over the top, ever. Low havoc by design; they'll concede 5-yard completions for sixty minutes and win the red zone.
s: Parker is the best pure DC in the sport, year in year out
Special teams wins them a game a season outright
Identity so stable it survives any roster
w: QB play caps the ceiling — again
Modern tempo offenses stress their substitution patterns

deep.intro:
2025: 9-4 (6-3), 12th in SP+ with the No. 6 defense — again. SP+: #22, 8.0 avg wins. 2026: Ferentz Year 28.

The gist. The most stable program in America faces its least stable roster: dead last in the Big Ten in returning production (104th nationally), with only six of 17 rotation defenders back and all four top TFL producers gone. Phil Parker hasn't ranked below sixth in defensive SP+ since 2018, so he gets the benefit of the doubt, but Ferentz signed eight defensive transfers — a franchise record by volume — many from the FCS ranks (Kahmari Brown, Anthony Hawkins, Xavier Styles) around holdovers Bryce Hawthorne, Jayden Montgomery, and Zach Lutmer. The offense is quietly the steadier unit under Tim Lester: RBs Kamari Moulton and Xavier Williams return, FCS star LJ Phillips Jr. (2,100+ scrimmage yards at South Dakota, a Connelly favorite) arrives, All-America G Kade Pieper anchors the line, and sophomore TE DJ Vonnahme was the most efficient per-route tight end in the country. The QB derby (Hank Brown vs. Jeremy Hecklinski) needs to clear only the modest bar Mark Gronowski left. Prediction: 8-4. Iowa is the sport's most reliable 8-4, and the Oct. trip by Ohio State to Kinnick is the annual rock-fight-of-the-year candidate. Ferentz's seat: geological.

Tennessee.ob: Heupel's Veer and Shoot — extreme splits, vertical stems, tempo that forbids substitution.
Tennessee.ot: Post-Aguilar QB room is open; the system's floor is high (spacing does the work) but its ceiling is QB-dependent. Run game is inside zone vs light boxes the splits create.
Tennessee.db: New DC Jim Knowles (via Penn State, and the 2024 natty-winning Ohio State defense before that) replaces Tim Banks, who left for Auburn's staff after the unit collapsed to #92 in scoring defense.
Tennessee.dt: Knowles brings his safety-driven 4-2-5 — late rotation, creative sim pressures, and a track record of immediate top-10 turnarounds. The scheme upgrade is the single biggest coordinator win of the SEC offseason.
s: Knowles is as good as defensive hires get
Scheme guarantees explosive plays regardless of personnel
OL continuity best of the Heupel era
w: QB competition with no proven answer
2025 defensive personnel didn't suddenly get faster — year-one Knowles fixes structure, not speed
Wide-split offense struggles vs elite two-high discipline

deep.intro: 2025: 8-5 (4-4) — a season-ending loss to Vanderbilt and a bowl loss to Illinois turned "fine" into "disappointing." SP+: #16, 7.7 avg wins. CBS: 8-4 (losses: Texas, Alabama, at South Carolina, at A&M, LSU). Vegas: 7.5 (Talty: under — the QB question scared him off), +2000.
deep.roster: The offense returns almost everything except the guy who threw it: leading rusher DeSean Bishop, twin 800-yard WRs Braylon Staley and Mike Matthews, and five of six linemen from a unit that ranked 7th in offensive SP+. But Joey Aguilar's 3,500-yard season is gone (his extra-year bid was denied — the anti-Chambliss), so it's redshirt freshman George MacIntyre vs. five-star true freshman Faizon Brandon: nine combined career passes. The defense face-planted from 6th to 63rd, so Heupel hired Jim Knowles from Penn State — his fourth defensive rebuild — and signed 12 defensive transfers. LBs Arion Carter and Jeremiah Telander return alongside Penn State's Amare Campbell; the secondary adds Kayin Lee (Auburn), TJ Metcalf (Michigan), and Qua Moss (K-State) around returning stars Ty Redmond and Edrees Farooq. Fresh update your listeners need: Penn State transfer edge Chaz Coleman — the headline defensive addition, a reported $2 million portal deal — was just dismissed from the team in July. The front is now the thinnest unit on the roster. Watch list: the QB derby (Brandon is the highest-ceiling freshman QB in the league), Bishop, Knowles' Year 1 chaos.
deep.identity: Offense: Heupel's warp-speed vertical spacing attack — proven pieces everywhere except the trigger. Defense: Knowles is aggressive and creative, but his Year 1s are historically glitchy (Penn State fell from 4th to 23rd in his one season there); he needs a year to place the chess pieces, and the roster gives him mismatched ones. Strengths: skill corps, O-line continuity, home schedule (Texas and LSU both come to Neyland). Weaknesses: QB inexperience, pass rush post-Coleman, a defense mid-rebuild, and eight projected toss-up games — the most volatile schedule shape in the league. Season goals: keep Heupel's floor at 8 wins while the freshman grows up; steal one of the marquee home games.
deep.prediction: 8-4 with the record hiding a fun team. If Brandon wins the job by October and hits, Tennessee is the 2027 preseason darling; the 2026 version is a year early. Heupel isn't listed on hot seat boards, but quietly: a QB whiff plus a 6-6 season in the year after losing to Vandy would change that conversation fast.

Michigan.ob: New era: Kyle Whittingham (hired from Utah after Sherrone Moore's December firing) inherits Bryce Underwood's year two — expect a physical, QB-run-friendly structure around the #1 recruit's arm.
Michigan.ot: Whittingham's Utah offenses were identity-stable: gap runs, play-action, low turnovers. Pairing that floor with Underwood's ceiling is the bet; the OC hire and system blend are the July unknowns — verify the staff before air.
Michigan.db: Wink Martindale's pressure-man — highest blitz-rate profile in the conference, corners on islands.
Michigan.dt: Boom-bust by design: elite havoc, occasional explosive concessions when the pressure loses. They erase bad QBs entirely.
s: Underwood's ceiling is the B1G's highest
Whittingham brings two decades of program-toughness credibility
Championship-grade DL two-deep survives the change
w: HC transition on top of a year-two QB — variance stacks
Whittingham has never coached east of the Rockies
WR separation remains the thinnest asset

deep.intro: 2025: 9-4 (7-2), 25th in SP+ — then the December implosion: Sherrone Moore fired for cause Dec. 10, arrested and charged days later. 2026 coach: Kyle Whittingham, first year — 21 seasons and a school-record 177 wins at Utah, hired Dec. 26 after Michigan's DeBoer pursuit fizzled. SP+: #14, 7.5 avg wins, No. 5 schedule. Vegas: +1500.
deep.roster: The crown jewel stayed: Bryce Underwood, the No. 1 recruit in the 2025 class, enters Year 2 after a freshman season of flashes (506 non-sack rushing yards on just 68 carries). Whittingham imported his Utah operating system wholesale — OC Jason Beck, who built a top-6 offense around dual-threat Devon Dampier last year, DC Jay Hill (from BYU via Utah lineage), O-line coach Jim Harding, and three Utah players: star DE John Henry Daley (a Connelly top-10 transfer, racing back from an October Achilles rupture), DT Jonah Lea'ea, and do-everything nickel Smith Snowden. Returning defensive core: DT Trey Pierce, LB Troy Bowles, CBs Jyaire Hill and Zeke Berry. The skill room is young: sophomore WR Andrew Marsh and RB Jordan Marshall are budding stars; transfers JJ Buchanan and Jaime Ffrench Jr. plus freshman Salesi Moa fill out a thin pass-catching group. Watch list: Underwood (the whole enterprise), Daley's Achilles, Marsh.
deep.identity: Offense: Beck's efficiency-first, QB-run-leveraged system — the fascinating question is whether he runs Underwood like he ran Dampier (who carried it twice as often) or builds something more conventional around a five-star arm. Defense: Hill's aggressive Utah/BYU structure with more raw talent than he's ever coached. Strengths: Underwood's ceiling, coaching pedigree, culture reset after a traumatic winter. Weaknesses: Connelly's two big flags — can a 66-year-old built on chip-on-the-shoulder rosters connect with blue-chippers, and does the Beck-Underwood marriage actually fit — plus receiver depth and the league's No. 5 schedule (Indiana visits Oct. 24; the Ohio State finale is in Columbus). Season goals: stability first, 9 wins second, and a healthy Underwood entering 2027 as the Heisman favorite.
deep.prediction: 8-4, competitive in everything, with the Underwood leap arriving in bursts rather than in full. The Whittingham hire is either a masterstroke bridge or an awkward era mismatch, and we genuinely won't know until October. No seat, obviously — but the administration that botched December needs this to look functional fast.

Washington.ob: Jedd Fisch pro-style spread — Demond Williams' dual-threat base, movement pockets, layered crossers.
Washington.ot: Williams quietly finished 2025 top-20 in EPA/play: the legs are real, and Fisch's route tree gives him easy first reads. OL rebuild is the throttle.
Washington.db: Multiple 4-2-5 — coverage-first, rally tackling, modest havoc.
Washington.dt: Competent, not scary: they limit explosives and force long drives, but generate little negative-play pressure without blitzing.
s: Williams is a rising top-10 QB nobody outside the West watches
w: Trench depth (both lines) below B1G contender grade
Pass rush needs a portal answer

deep.intro: 2025: 9-4 (5-4) — but 0-4 against the four best defenses they played (9.3 PPG in those games), 45.1 PPG against everyone else. SP+: #21, 7.8 avg wins. Vegas: +3000.
deep.roster: QB Demond Williams Jr.'s offseason was a saga — signed, briefly left for the portal, came back calling the departure a mistake — but he's the engine: 7.5 yards per dropback, 7.3 per carry, massive upside, too many sacks. Four line starters return plus Sam Houston transfer Kolt Dieterich; the skill room lost leading rusher Jonah Coleman and WR Denzel Boston, replaced by sophomores (Jordan Washington, Dezmen Roebuck, Chris Lawson) and vets Jayden Limar (Oregon) and Christian Moss. Ryan Walters' defense surged 36th→9th in Year 1 and returns keys at every level (Elinneus Davis, Jacob Lane, Xe'ree Alexander, Alex McLaughlin, three sophomore DBs) — but Davis is the only returning tackle of six who logged 100+ snaps, so transfers Kai McClendon, Darin Conley, and DeSean Watts must hit. Watch list: Williams, Roebuck, Lane.
deep.identity: Fisch's pro-style with a jet-fueled QB; Walters' aggressive defense. Strength: everything against normal teams. Weakness: everything against great defenses — and the 2026 slate serves Iowa, Penn State, Indiana (Nov. 21, Connelly's sneaky-huge game), and Oregon. Goals: solve one elite defense and the CFP conversation opens.
deep.prediction: 8-4 with a genuine upset in the bag — this profile (blowout artist vs. the middle class, stuck vs. the elite) usually breaks through exactly once a year. Fisch is building something real; Year 3 tells us the ceiling.

Missouri.ob: Drinkwitz spread — Ahmad Hardy's downhill volume behind heavy pre-snap motion, Beau Pribula-era RPO game.
Missouri.ot: Hardy led the nation in rushing as a sophomore; everything constraints off him. Austin Simmons arrives from Ole Miss to settle the QB room — an SEC-experienced arm to keep defenses out of nine-man boxes.
Missouri.db: Corey Batoon's 4-2-5 — spot-drop discipline, interior push over edge speed.
Missouri.dt: Top-25 success rate allowed in 2025; the profile is stout-vs-run, adequate-vs-pass. They keep games in the 20s.
s: Hardy is the SEC's best returning RB — full stop
Home environment consistently banks an upset
w: QB clarity needed before the identity fully clicks
Ceiling historically caps at 10 wins — breaking through needs variance

deep.intro: 2025: 8-5 (4-4). Only three SEC programs have more wins than Mizzou's 29 over the past three years. SP+: #19, 7.3 avg wins. CBS: 6-6 — Crawford's pick for the biggest loser of the nine-game schedule. Vegas: 6.5 (Talty: under), +3500.
deep.roster: Three questions define the season, per Connelly's framing. One: Ahmad Hardy — the All-American back who ran for 1,649 yards — was shot in the upper leg leaving a concert in May, and his return timetable is unknown. Backup Jamal Roberts is one of the country's best RB2s and FCS 1,000-yard rusher Xai'Shaun Edwards arrives, but Mizzou's ceiling travels with Hardy's rehab. Two: QB Austin Simmons (Ole Miss) — the lefty with the prettiest deep ball in the transfer class, who started 2025 as Ole Miss's QB1, got Wally Pipp'd by Chambliss after 84 dropbacks, and flashed 15 twenty-yard completions against five picks in that tiny sample. New OC Chip Lindsey's whole job is keeping the big plays and killing the mistakes. Three: the pass rush — all three sack leaders are gone, the secondary lost seven of eight and re-armed with Oregon and Ole Miss transfers (Jahlil Florence, Sione Laulea, Chris Graves Jr.), and the whole structure only works if QBs stay harassed. Connelly's favorite under-the-radar transfer: Auburn LB Robert Woodyard Jr. (14.7% tackle rate) next to speedster Nicholas Rodriguez. Watch list: Hardy's health above everything, Simmons, sophomore WR Donovan Olugbode.
deep.identity: Offense: Drinkwitz's run-anchored play-action game with a new vertical dimension if Simmons clicks. Defense: 7th nationally in yards per dropback last year — pressure-and-coverage synergy that must be rebuilt on both ends. Strengths: O-line experience, DT pair (Gracial/Marshall), LB spine, program stability. Weaknesses: pass rush, secondary turnover, RB uncertainty, and a schedule that swapped Big 12-lite crossovers for Texas, Oklahoma, and a trip to Georgia. Season goals: keep the top-20 program floor intact through a retooling year; get Hardy back whole.
deep.prediction: 7-5, splitting the difference — this program has out-kicked projections three straight years and 6-6 feels disrespectful, but the schedule jump is real. Drinkwitz's seat is ice cold (1.11 on the last published CBS temperature scale, effectively untouchable).

SMU.ob: Rhett Lashlee tempo-RPO spread — wide splits, snap-count warfare, RB screens as extended runs.
SMU.ot: Kevin Jennings' era likely closes; the system has produced a top-25 offense three straight years with three different QBs. Expect the same: pace, spacing, and YAC manufacturing.
SMU.db: 3-3-5 pressure-sim front — transfer-heavy back seven, gambling profile.
SMU.dt: Havoc-rich, bust-prone: they'll win you three games with takeaways and lose you one with explosives. ACC offenses solved stretches of 2025.
s: System offense with proven QB plug-and-play
Dallas NIL base keeps the roster stocked
Lashlee's in-game tempo manipulation is elite
w: Defensive floor is the concern in big games
Line play still a tier below ACC's best
Post-CFP expectations meet a harder schedule

deep.intro: Coach: Rhett Lashlee, Year 5 (38-17). Secure — 14-2 in ACC regular-season play since joining, 31 wins over three years.
deep.roster: The great news: Kevin Jennings returns as a third-year starting QB, a genuine rarity. The sobering news: only eight returning starters, the top four yards-from-scrimmage producers are gone, and seven defensive starters left. Portal class was lean by design (15 in, ~two dozen out — a sign of roster health): RB Kendrick Raphael (Cal), WRs Yannick Smith (ECU) and Jalen Hale (Alabama), TEs Randy Pittman Jr. (FSU) and Theo Melin Öhrström (Texas A&M), edges Marques White (UMass, 10.5 TFL) and Ira Singleton (USF), nickel Jarvis Lee (USF, 9.5 TFL), plus 305-pound-plus DTs Christian Davis (La. Tech) and Malcolm Alcorn-Crowder (K-State). Returners to watch: All-ACC OT PJ Williams fronts a line that needed zero portal help (a luxury almost nobody else in this league has), slot Yamir Knight, sophomores Derrick McFall (RB) and Jalen Cooper (WR), three returning LBs (Booker, Miyazono, Kilgore) and three returning corners.
deep.identity: Lashlee's tempo spread, now run by a committee — he lost both coordinators (OC Casey Woods to the Missouri State HC job, DC Scott Symons to the Cowboys) and responded by naming co-coordinator troikas: Rob Likens/Garin Justice/D'Eriq King on offense, Maurice Crum Jr./Rickey Hunley Jr. on defense. Either supreme cultural confidence or overthinking — that's a live pod debate. Jennings is the whole personality: in 26 starts, SMU is 12-1 when his QBR clears 76 and 3-4 when it dips under 50. Strengths: QB continuity, the O-line, linebacker depth. Weaknesses: brand-new skill corps chemistry, a defense in transition post-Symons, and special teams (102nd last year). Goal: get back to Charlotte after blowing it with kick-yourself losses at Wake (13-12) and Cal (38-35) — beat either and they'd have played for the title.
deep.prediction: CBS's boldest call in the league — 11-1, ACC runner-up, No. 10 CFP seed, aided by a schedule with no Miami and early road tests at FSU and Louisville. SP+ is cooler (8.0 wins). The Sept. 19 trip to Louisville is the biggest non-Miami game on the ACC calendar. Coach status: completely safe.

Auburn.ob: Alex Golesh's tempo, TE-friendly vertical spread arrives from USF — OC Joel Gordon came with him (his play-caller all three Tampa years; #2 national total offense in 2025).
Auburn.ot: Golesh offenses live in 12-personnel spacing at Veer-and-Shoot pace — the exact inverse of the Freeze stagnation. Staff assembled in 17 days: Kodi Burns (WRs, Auburn alum), Larry Porter (RBs), Tim Banks (safeties, from Tennessee). QB room portal-rebuilt (Deuce Knight left for Ole Miss).
Auburn.db: Continuity where it counts: DJ Durkin RETAINED as DC — the fronts, sim pressures, and personnel carry over intact.
Auburn.dt: Keeping Durkin means the defense skips the reinstall tax entirely. DL talent accumulation is top-10 nationally; the 2025 defense was better than its record.
s: Golesh's offense fixes the exact thing that got Freeze fired
Durkin retention = one-sided transition, not a teardown
Jordan-Hare in year-one-energy mode is worth a home upset
w: Full identity reinstall on offense — year-one variance is huge
QB is a total unknown after the Knight exit
SEC schedule shows no mercy to transitions

deep.intro: 2025: 5-7 (1-7) — Freeze fired in November after losing 12 of his last 13 one-score games, an almost supernatural run of pain. 2026 coach: Alex Golesh, Year 1 — from USF, and he brought 11 Bulls with him. SP+: #26, 6.5 avg wins. CBS: 7-5 and a bowl (Talty: over at -118). Vegas: 6.5, +4500.
deep.roster: The centerpiece: QB Byrum Brown, who followed Golesh from USF after a 3,158-pass / 1,121-rush / 42-TD season — Connelly ranks him among his favorite transfers anywhere and among the best QBs in the Power 4.
