The supporting cast is half-Tampa: RB Nykahi Davenport and four USF receivers (Keshaun Singleton, Christian Neptune, Jeremiah Koger, Chas Nimrod) join returning back Jeremiah Cobb and Baylor's Bryson Washington. The line is the gamble — top five linemen gone, nine transfers asked to cohere. Defense: DC D.J. Durkin was retained after back-to-back top-20 units, but only two of the ten 300-snap defenders return; the reload leans on sophomores (Jared Smith, Xavier Atkins, Elijah Melendez, Kaleb Harris) plus transfers Da'Shawn Womack (Ole Miss), Cody Sigler (Arkansas State — 8 TFLs at 302 pounds, another Connelly favorite), and corners Andre Jordan Jr. and Shamar Arnoux. Notable exits: Cam Coleman (Texas), Eric Singleton Jr. (Florida), Kayin Lee (Tennessee) — the old regime's skill talent scattered to rivals. Watch list: Brown, Sigler, Cobb.
deep.identity: Offense: Golesh's up-tempo, explosive spread — he lifted USF from 114th to 30th in SP+ in three years on the strength of it. Defense: Durkin continuity, new bodies. Strengths: QB, coaching-schematic clarity, motivated-transfer energy. Weaknesses: both lines are transfer science experiments, and the ghost: seven projected one-score games for a program that just lost 12 of 13 of them. Season goals: a bowl, close-game normalcy, and making the Iron Bowl matter again.
deep.prediction: 7-5 and the feel-good Year 1 story — Brown is too good, and close-game luck regresses to the mean by force of statistics. Golesh has no seat, but Auburn's last three coaches would like a word about how fast that changes on the Plains.

South Florida.ob: Brian Hartline era — Ohio State's OC/WR-whisperer took the job in December 2025. Expect a pro-spread with elite receiver development replacing Golesh's tempo-vertical system.
South Florida.ot: Byrum Brown's graduation plus the system change makes this a double reset, but Hartline is a Tampa-area legend recruiting his own backyard. Final season at Raymond James before the on-campus stadium opens in 2027 — momentum is real even in transition.
South Florida.db: The 2025 defense was aggressive, multiple, and turnover-hungry — how much survives the staff turnover is the open question.
South Florida.dt: USF punched above its weight through scheme and culture; the architect left, but the administration's investment (and Hartline's recruiting pull) suggests a reload, not a collapse. Grade the two-deep in September, not July.
s: Hartline is arguably the best WR developer in football, college or pro
Tampa recruiting footprint is genuinely fertile — and now has a closer
Administration showed it will invest after the CFP near-miss
w: First-time head coach replacing a proven one, with a new QB
System change on offense means year-one friction
FPI's #25 is a lagging indicator — treat with maximum skepticism

LSU.ob: Lane Kiffin's tempo-RPO spread arrives in Baton Rouge — the same pace-and-glance system that just produced Ole Miss's 13-win CFP season, now with LSU's talent base.
LSU.ot: The portal answer landed: Sam Leavitt from Arizona State — the biggest spring QB move in the country. Kiffin + Leavitt + LSU skill talent is a CFP-or-bust construction; Nussmeier is off to the draft.
LSU.db: Multiple 4-2-5 rebuild under Kiffin's hires — verify the coordinator before air; the transition consumed the winter.
LSU.dt: The 2025 defense underachieved amid the Brian Kelly firing chaos — talent has outrun results in Baton Rouge for two years. Kiffin's Ole Miss defenses were portal-patched and opportunistic; expect the same blueprint.
s: Leavitt is a proven CFP-caliber QB in a proven system
NIL/portal muscle to patch any hole fast
w: Leavitt must gel with a new OL fast — install runway is short
Mid-season firing chaos tax: two systems, two staffs in one year
OL was the 2025 failure point and returns unproven

deep.intro: 2025: 7-6 (3-5) — Kelly fired in October. 2026 coach: Lane Kiffin, Year 1. SP+: #10, 8.5 avg wins. CBS: 10-2, No. 9 CFP seed — with the delicious detail that the two losses come at Ole Miss and at Tennessee, his two former SEC schools. Vegas: 8.5 (Talty: over, -150), +850.
deep.roster: The No. 1 transfer portal class in America, full stop — the self-styled Portal King's magnum opus after four straight top-five classes at Ole Miss. The headliners: QB Sam Leavitt (Arizona State — the portal's No. 1 QB, the 2024 ASU playoff-run engine before injuries wrecked his 2025; Kiffin literally flew to meet him to close the deal), plus heir apparent Husan Longstreet (USC five-star). Four explosive transfer WRs: Jayce Brown (Kansas State, a monstrous 3.0 yards per route run), Jackson Harris (Hawai'i), Tre' Brown III (ODU), Winston Watkins (Ole Miss) — plus Eugene Wilson III poached from Florida. OT Jordan Seaton (Colorado — zero sacks allowed, 1.1% blown-block rate) anchors a rebuilt line. On defense: Ole Miss edge Princewill Umanmielen (portal's No. 1 edge), Tennessee edge Jordan Ross, Auburn DT Malik Blocton, Ole Miss LB TJ Dottery, Boise State S Ty Benefield (105 tackles) join returnees Whit Weeks, Dominick McKinley, DJ Pickett, and Harold Perkins' departed shadow. Blue-chip freshmen Lamar Brown, Richard Anderson, and Havon Finney Jr. give it depth. Returning production: just 61%, 89 returning starts — this is a build-a-bear roster. Watch list: Leavitt, Umanmielen, TE Trey'Dez Green (the unlock candidate), Weeks.
deep.identity: Offense: Kiffin/Charlie Weis Jr. tempo-and-leverage passing — Kiffin coaxes top-25 offense out of nearly any QB, and Leavitt is far better than "any QB." Defense: Blake Baker's aggressive pressure system — two top-15 SP+ defenses in the past three years between Mizzou and LSU, and SP+ projects this unit No. 2 nationally. Strengths: pass-catcher explosiveness, defensive front talent, coordinator pedigree on both sides. Weaknesses: O-line depth is genuinely precarious (only two of last year's top seven back), unproven RBs (Durham and Berry haven't matched the recruiting hype), and total roster cohesion risk — new coach, new QB, 136 incoming starts. Season goals: LSU hasn't finished top-10 in SP+ since the 2019 title season. Kiffin was not hired to compete for playoff spots; he was hired for championships, and CBS says no first-year coach in America faces more pressure.
deep.prediction: The schedule front-loads the drama: Clemson in Week 1, at Ole Miss Week 3, A&M Week 4, and the closing stretch brings Alabama and Texas to Baton Rouge. I'll say 9-3 and a bubble sweat rather than CBS's clean 10-2 — transfer-heavy teams usually leak one game they shouldn't. But the ceiling is a title, and the floor (7-5 with a locker-room chemistry story) would be the loudest story in the sport. Hot seat: obviously not in Year 1 — but Kiffin has imported championship-now expectations, a hated-rival backstory, and a fan base that fired Kelly at 7-5ish pace. There is no honeymoon clause in that contract.

South Carolina.ob: Shane Beamer's spread built around LaNorris Sellers — designed QB run, play-action verticals, the offense flowing entirely through #16.
South Carolina.ot: Sellers RETURNED — a projected top-5 NFL pick who chose Columbia over the draft, the single biggest returning-player win in the conference. 2025 wasted him behind poor protection; the whole offseason was spent fixing his supporting cast.
South Carolina.db: 4-2-5 with the edge-rush pipeline the program has become known for — speed off both edges, safeties downhill.
South Carolina.dt: Better than its 2025 record: the defense kept games winnable while the offense sputtered. Front-seven athleticism is SEC-caliber; depth behind it is not.
s: Sellers is arguably the SEC's best player at any position
Beamer's special teams steal hidden points
w: The OL must stop getting Sellers hit — same sentence as last July
Skill weapons a tier below the SEC's best
Staff pressure: wasting Sellers' return is a fireable offense

deep.intro: 2025: 4-8 (1-7) — a preseason top-13 team that blew a 30-3 halftime lead to Texas A&M and rattled off a five-game midseason losing streak. SP+: #25, 6.6 avg wins. CBS: 7-5 (with the Georgia upset! Crawford picks the Gamecocks to hand UGA its only regular-season loss). Vegas: 6.5 (Talty: under), +5500.
deep.roster: The two most physically gifted players in the conference at their positions are both back: QB LaNorris Sellers and WR Nyck Harbor — Connelly calls them the most intimidating pitch-and-catch combo in the country — plus edge Dylan Stewart, the third member of the "ridiculously talented, frustratingly inconsistent" club. The problem: only six offensive players who saw 200+ snaps return. Beamer responded with a full teardown — fourth OC in five years (Kendal Briles from TCU), eight new offensive linemen (five FBS starters, six seniors), three new RBs led by Texas blue-chipper Christian Clark, and four transfer WRs (Nitro Tuggle, DJ Black — both with drop-rate red flags, same as Harbor). Defense: sixth-year DC Clayton White returns 10 of 19 starters; the portal adds Tennessee edge Caleb Herring, Illinois DT Tomiwa Durojaiye, and ex-five-star Kelby Collins (Alabama). LB Justin Okoronkwo, S Peyton Williams, and electric CB/PR Vicari Swain (three punt-return TDs) hold the spine. 68% returning production — 5th nationally, the best in the SEC. Watch list: Sellers (a top-5 NFL draft profile if it clicks), Stewart, Swain.
deep.identity: Offense: Briles brings tempo and vertical shots — theoretically the perfect marriage for Sellers' arm and Harbor's 4.2 speed, if the rebuilt line holds and Sellers stops running himself into disaster sacks. Defense: negative-play creation without a finishing pass rush (97th in sack rate) — Stewart converting talent into sacks is the whole equation. Strengths: top-end talent, continuity metrics, even-year mysticism (Beamer is 17-9 in even years, 9-15 in odd ones — genuinely all the analysis some people use). Weaknesses: O-line cohesion, drops everywhere, close-game execution, and zero margin in the schedule. Season goals: bowl minimum, and honestly: save the job.
deep.prediction: The hot seat — full treatment. How he got here: five years of violent oscillation — 7-of-9 win streaks and 1-6 collapses, a near-CFP 2024 followed by a 4-8 2025 that included the A&M meltdown. USA TODAY's league-wide hot seat piece puts him first, notes he survived 2025 largely because half the conference was already conducting searches, and quotes him acknowledging the town expects a winner. How he escapes: 7-5 probably buys a Year 7 given the schedule; 8-4 with the Clemson or Georgia scalp makes him a hero again. The even-year pattern, the Sellers/Stewart/Harbor triangle, and the best returning-production profile in the league are all genuinely on his side. How it ends:** another October spiral, a September home loss to a mid-tier opponent, or Sellers regressing behind a leaky line — any of those and the search fires up before Thanksgiving. My call: 7-5, survival, and this exact conversation again in 2027.

Florida.ob: Jon Sumrall (from Tulane) brings his physical, run-first spread — QB run game, heavy play-action, ball-control identity.
Florida.ot: DJ Lagway left for Baylor after the coaching change, so the QB is a portal answer running a brand-new system. Sumrall's Tulane teams won with discipline, turnover margin, and fourth-down conviction — the exact traits late-Napier Florida lacked.
Florida.db: Sumrall is a defense-and-culture head coach: aggressive 4-2-5, tackling-first, simplified assignments.
Florida.dt: The roster has more SEC talent than its 2025 results showed — misused, not missing. Expect immediate physicality gains and early-season simplicity.
s: Sumrall has the best culture track record of any recent mid-major riser
DL and secondary talent stockpiled from top-10 classes
Roster is battle-hardened by the league's toughest schedules
w: QB reset after Lagway's exit — new system, new triggerman
Second offensive system in two years for the returners
The Swamp does not grade on a transition curve

deep.intro: 2025: 4-8 (2-6) — Napier fired after a 3-4 start, DJ Lagway led the SEC in interceptions, the season died early. 2026 coach: Jon Sumrall, Year 1 — hired from Tulane, where he coached the Green Wave INTO the CFP (losing the first-round game at Ole Miss) after accepting the Florida job. SP+: #20, 7.0 avg wins. CBS: 6-6 (Talty: under on 7.5). Vegas: 7.5, +2200.
deep.roster: The QB story dominates: Lagway entered the portal in December and, after a chaotic recruitment (an FSU visit! an Ole Miss visit! a wobbling Baylor commitment!), signed with Baylor. The room is now RS freshman Aaron Philo — a Georgia Tech transfer reunited with his old OC, Buster Faulkner — plus true freshmen Tramell Jones and Will Griffin. The non-QB roster is sneaky good: Sumrall kept RB Jadan Baugh (1,170 yards), added Auburn's Eric Singleton Jr. to a young WR room (Vernell Brown III, Dallas Wilson, TJ Abrams), and inherits a defense with real pieces — LBs Myles Graham and Jaden Robinson, S Bryce Thornton, breakout sophomore edge Jayden Woods, DT Brendan Bett, and a deep vault of enormous defensive tackles (the one thing Napier reliably stockpiled). New DC Brad White (from Kentucky) adds transfers Emmanuel Oyebadejo, DJ Coleman, and Jordy Lowery. The one big loss besides Lagway: WR Eugene Wilson III, poached by Kiffin. 66% returning production (11th nationally) — the quiet continuity play. Watch list: Philo, Baugh, Woods, Cormani McClain's redemption arc.
deep.identity: Offense: Sumrall/Faulkner physicality-first with a downhill run game — exactly Baugh's profile. Defense: White inherits a unit that already rushed the passer well on passing downs; this should be good fast. Strengths: Sumrall's track record is absurd (Troy from 10-13 to 23-5; 20 wins, an American title, and a CFP berth in two Tulane years), run game, DT depth, and — rare for Florida — a manageable schedule: Texas and Georgia back-to-back, but only one other projected top-15 opponent, and the FSU rivalry game comes against a rebuilding opponent. Weaknesses: QB inexperience, WR proven production, the usual Year 1 install tax. Season goals: bowl comfortably, beat FSU, and land one signature scalp (CBS has them beating Oklahoma).
deep.prediction: I'm with SP+ over CBS here: 7-5 with an upset that makes Gainesville believe again. Sumrall's floor at every stop has been "immediately better," and this roster is closer than a 4-8 record suggests. No seat in Year 1 — but Florida has burned through three coaches in a decade, so the honeymoon meter runs faster there than anywhere.

Mississippi State.ob: Jeff Lebby's year-three tempo spread (Veer-and-Shoot family) around rising QB KaMario Taylor — vertical shots off wide splits at pace.
Mississippi State.ot: Taylor is the league's quiet breakout pick, with Kevin Johns (from Oklahoma State) hired to coach the QB room. Scoring won't be the problem; it never is in Lebby's offense by year three.
Mississippi State.db: Rebuilt defensive staff for 2026 — the 2025 unit was among the SEC's worst on early downs.
Mississippi State.dt: They'll score and get scored on: the win total rides entirely on the defense climbing from bad to mediocre. Watch shootout unders... which is to say, overs.
s: Taylor's trajectory is real — dual-threat with a live arm
Lebby's offense travels regardless of talent gaps
Year-three continuity is rare currency in this league's chaos
w: Defense still bottom-tier SEC after two rebuild years
Two-deep depth thins fast at contact positions
Zero margin for error in this schedule

deep.intro: 2025: 5-8 (1-7) — a 4-0 start, a 1-8 finish, but with OT scares of Tennessee and Texas and an upset of ranked Arizona State. SP+: #52, 4.5 avg wins. CBS: 4-8, one SEC win (Auburn). Vegas: 4.5 (Talty: over, -115).
deep.roster: Sophomore QB Kamario Taylor is the reason for hope: an 82.3 QBR in a two-start, 106-dropback sample — fourth-in-the-league numbers if they qualified — with big-play hunting, real rushing, and way too many sacks. Backups AJ Swann and fallen five-star Jaden Rashada arrive as insurance. The line is the terror: six of the top seven gone, eight transfers in, most of them young. Skill continuity is decent (Fluff Bothwell, Anthony Evans III, Ayden Williams, Missouri transfer Marquis Johnson). The defensive twist: Zach Arnett is back in Starkville as DC — yes, the same Arnett who succeeded Leach as head coach and was fired mid-season, now working for the guy who replaced him. Four of five returning D-linemen led by 315-lb playmaker Jaray Bledsoe, CB Kelley Jones, and six transfer DBs. Watch list: Taylor (a genuine 2027 breakout candidate), Bledsoe.
deep.identity: Offense: Lebby tempo — the unit jumped from 82nd to 39th in offensive SP+ last year, which is the entire case for keeping him. Defense: never came around; Arnett's return is a culture-and-scheme reset. Strengths: QB upside, DL interior, low expectations. Weaknesses: O-line, pass rush, secondary churn, and a murderer's row — six consecutive projected top-25 opponents starting Week 3. Season goals: steal one ranked scalp at Davis-Wade, get to four or five wins, show Taylor's arrow.
deep.prediction: The hot seat — full treatment. How he got here: 7-18 overall, one SEC win in two seasons, and a fan base with the itchiest trigger finger in the league — the school fired Joe Moorhead after consecutive winning seasons and didn't let Arnett finish one full year. Lebby's buyout is described as manageable, which is the most dangerous adjective in coaching. The uncomfortable structural question, straight from the league's hot-seat coverage: does MSU employ a head coach, or two coordinators (Lebby and Arnett) with no head coach between them? How he escapes: an upset of Alabama or Oklahoma in Starkville, 3-4 SEC wins, visible Taylor development — any real November relevance and the trajectory argument (2-10 → 5-8 → up) carries him to Year 4. How it ends:** 1-8 in the league again, and the Arnett-in-the-building succession plan writes itself. My call: 5-7, genuinely improved, and a coin-flip December decision.

Kentucky.ob: Will Stein — Oregon's offensive coordinator — takes over after Kentucky moved on from Mark Stoops following back-to-back losing seasons. His motion-heavy wide-zone spread replaces a decade of grind-it-out identity.
Kentucky.ot: Stein was the hottest OC hire on the market; the roster he inherits was built for the opposite style. Expect a portal-heavy skill infusion and a year-one offense that looks modern immediately but runs thin by November.
Kentucky.db: The Stoops-era defensive identity leaves with him — new coordinator, new structure (verify the staff before air).
Kentucky.dt: Kentucky's floor was always its defense under Stoops; that floor is now unknown. The rebuild is total, both sides.
s: Stein's offense is the anti-Stoops — pace, space, and QB-friendly structure
Expectations at rock bottom: everything is upside
First-mover credibility with portal offensive skill
w: Full-program reset — scheme, staff, and roster simultaneously
Roster was recruited for a different sport, stylistically
The SEC schedule doesn't wait for installs

deep.intro: 2025: 5-7 (2-6) — the quiet end of the Mark Stoops era after a two-year slide. 2026 coach: Will Stein, Year 1 — hired off Oregon's staff and building the transfer class while coordinating the Ducks' CFP semifinal run in January. SP+: #54, 4.2 avg wins (No. 8 schedule). CBS: 5-7 (Talty: under at +118). Vegas: 4.5.
deep.roster: Stein's transfer class could start 12-15 players immediately. The offense: Notre Dame QB Kenny Minchey (another small-sample darling — accurate, mobile, unproven), a giant backfield of CJ Baxter (Texas) and Jovantae Barnes (Oklahoma), and six transfer linemen averaging 6-5, 323 with 49 combined FBS starts — three of them all-conference honorees (Lance Heard, Tegra Tshabola, Mark Robinson). The receiver room is the void: TE Willie Rodriguez is the only sure thing. Defense: the strength — 10 of 19 game-starters return (Mi'Quise Humphrey-Grace, Ty Bryant, Terhyon Nichols, Grant Grayton), reinforced by 13 transfers including Florida S Jordan Castell. Watch list: Minchey, Baxter (the former five-star recruit rebooting), the mauling new line.
deep.identity: Offense: Stein's Oregon-style spacing and motion game, run through a power line — an odd and interesting hybrid. Defense: veteran, solid, unspectacular. Strengths: OL mass, RB room, defensive continuity, coaching upside. Weaknesses: WR talent, QB proof, and a top-10 schedule with almost no soft SEC draws. Season goals: 5-6 wins with obvious offensive identity would be a triumphant Year 1.
deep.prediction: 5-7 but frisky — the kind of team that ruins someone's November. Stein might be the best long-game hire of the cycle; Connelly outright says he wouldn't be surprised if they beat projections.

Arkansas.ob: Ryan Silverfield (50-25 at Memphis, two 10-win seasons) brings a balanced pro-spread with a strong OL-development reputation.
Arkansas.ot: The offense will be built around efficiency and the run game while the portal sorts the QB room. Silverfield's Memphis teams were clean: low turnovers, high red-zone conversion, few beat-yourself losses.
Arkansas.db: New staff, fundamentals-first rebuild — reports pointed at an in-state coordinator hire; verify before air.
Arkansas.dt: The 2025 defense was bottom-quartile SEC and the talent gap is real. Year one is about structure, not stars.
s: Silverfield develops OL and culture — the two things Arkansas lacked
A genuinely fresh start after the Pittman plateau
Soft on-ramp: opens with North Alabama Sept 5
w: Talent gap vs even the SEC's middle class

deep.intro: 2025: 2-10 (0-8) — winless in the league with a top-20 offense, which tells you everything about the defense. Pittman fired in September. 2026 coach: Ryan Silverfield, Year 1 from Memphis (never a losing season in six years there). SP+: #46, 4.3 avg wins — with the No. 2 schedule in America. CBS: 4-8 (Talty: under, -144).
deep.roster: A near-total teardown: only three of sixteen 200-snap defenders return, and just one mattered — DL Quincy Rhodes Jr. and his 17.5 TFLs, the lone keeper from the country's most broken defense. New DC Ron Roberts inherits 20+ defensive transfers and four juco adds: disruptive DBs (Jahiem Johnson, La'khi Roland, John Howse IV, Christian Harrison), a wall of 300-pounders, and LB bets Khmori House (UNC) and Ja'Quavion Smith. Offense: a two-man QB derby between sophomore KJ Jackson and blue-chip Memphis import AJ Hill, with Memphis RB Sutton Smith and Boise's Chris Marshall the proven skill pieces, and a seven-man OL transfer haul needing to produce three starters. Watch list: Rhodes, the QB battle, Silverfield's play-calling identity transplant.
deep.identity: Offense: Silverfield's Memphis attacks averaged a top-16 offensive SP+ ranking over his last four years — he can score. Defense: his Memphis defenses averaged 89th, and he inherited the worst unit in the power conferences. That asymmetry is the whole evaluation. Strengths: offensive scheme, low bar, no pressure. Weaknesses: the entire defensive two-deep is a grab bag, and the schedule (at Utah, Georgia, at A&M, Tennessee, at Texas, LSU...) is legitimately the hardest rebuild draw imaginable. Season goals: 4 wins, a real QB answer, one SEC scalp.
deep.prediction: 3-9, better than the record, with a signature near-miss that sells hope. No seat in Year 1; Arkansas just wants a pulse and stability after three 2-10 seasons in eight years.

Illinois.ob: Bielema's pro-style spread — play-action heavy, TE-friendly, built on OL development.
Illinois.ot: Luke Altmyer's graduation opens the QB room after the winningest three-year run in program history. The formula stays: protect the ball, win the hidden yards, play from ahead.
Illinois.db: 4-2-5 two-high shell — takeaway-driven, disciplined, rarely blitz-reliant.
Illinois.dt: Consistently top-25 in turnover margin under Bielema; they concede yards between the 20s and clamp in the red zone.
w: Skill speed below B1G contender grade

deep.intro:
2025: 9-4, powered by the FBS-best 13 one-score wins over three years (.765 win rate in them). SP+: #32, 7.0 avg wins.

The gist. The close-game machine loses its trigger: Luke Altmyer's three-year run ends, replaced by East Carolina transfer Katin Houser (13-6 as a starter, statistically Altmyer-like, fewer sacks, less scramble juice). The receiving corps refreshes with FIU's Alex Perry and FAU's Jayshon Platt around Hudson Clement; all three lead backs return (Kaden Feagin now at TE); but only one O-line starter is back. The defense is the bigger renovation — Montana legend Bobby Hauck takes over as coordinator with only four of 15 front-six regulars returning; the secondary (Xavier Scott back healthy, Matthew Bailey, Tanner Heckel) is the strength. Bielema's confidence is a weapon, but Connelly doubts the front-six personnel. Prediction: 7-5 — the one-score luck regresses at the exact moment the roster resets. Bielema's seat is cool; the program's floor is his legacy.

Maryland.ob: Locksley's pass-first spread around Malik Washington — the true-freshman revelation of 2025 returns as the conference's most exciting young arm.
Maryland.ot: Washington threw it 40+ times a game down the stretch; expect a more balanced year two with a rebuilt OL in front of him.
Maryland.db: 4-2-5 with rebuilt back seven — 2025 collapsed late-season yet again.
Maryland.dt: The pattern is Maryland's curse: competitive Septembers, brutal Novembers. The defense, not Washington, decides if that changes.
s: Washington is a future star — genuine top-15-pick trajectory
w: Defense a tier below the offense's arc

deep.intro: 2025: 4-8, second straight. SP+: #53, 5.8 wins — but No. 2 NATIONALLY in returning production. This is the all-in year Locksley designed: he handed blue-chip freshman QB Malik Washington the keys in 2025, ate the losses, kept him out of the portal, and returns 13 of 19 defensive regulars including a monster sophomore edge duo (Sidney Stewart + Zahir Mathis: 20.5 TFLs as freshmen). Washington's flaws were real (15th of 17 B1G QBs in QBR, heat-seeking fastballs off receivers' hands) but so was the sack avoidance and scrambling; new OC Clint Trickett and transfer targets (Chris Durr Jr., Na'eem Abdul-Rahim Gladding) are the support package. Hot seat, full treatment: Year 8, 37-49, under .500 across any window you pick — the development-year gamble only pays if 2026 produces a bowl and visible Washington growth. How he escapes: 6-6+ and a top-40 defense; the roster genuinely supports it. How it ends: another 4-8 with this much continuity would remove every argument. Prediction: 6-6, bowl, survival by a nose.

Michigan State.ob: Pat Fitzgerald returns to coaching in East Lansing after Jonathan Smith's firing — expect his identity: physical inside run, play-action, field position.
Michigan State.ot: Fitzgerald's Northwestern teams won with structure and special teams, never talent. MSU gives him more raw material than Evanston ever did; the QB room is unsettled.
Michigan State.db: 4-3-quarters roots — sound, physical, turnover-hunting. Staff assembly ongoing; verify coordinators before air.
Michigan State.dt: The floor rises immediately (Fitzgerald teams don't beat themselves); the ceiling depends on portal talent acquisition catching up.
s: Fresh energy after two failed rebuilds
B1G-caliber facilities and NIL waiting to be used
w: Roster talent bottom-third of the B1G
Fitzgerald's last CFB game was 2022 — rust is real

deep.intro: 2025: 4-8; Jonathan Smith fired after going 4-15 across parts of two seasons. 2026: Pat Fitzgerald, hired the very next day — a move Connelly graded harshly and stands by: a coach who went 4-20 in his last two Northwestern seasons handed a big-time job with no interim proving ground. SP+: #67, 4.4 wins. The plan: run the ball (UConn's 1,240-yard Cam Edwards leads four transfer RBs) and steal possessions (transfer DBs Tre Bell, Tyran Chappell, Michael Richard, Devin Vaught combined for 12 INTs). The problems: sophomore Alessio Milivojevic starts at QB mostly by default, the receiver room has no proven playmakers, and depth everywhere is a coin flip. Prediction: 4-8. Even Connelly's most generous read is "if Fitzgerald succeeds here, it probably won't be in 2026."

Minnesota.ob: Fleck's run-first, boat-rowing ball control — outside zone, max-protect shots, Drake Lindsey managing.
Minnesota.ot: Lindsey's sophomore year showed enough to keep the job; the identity never changes: shorten the game, win 4th quarters with the OL.
Minnesota.db: 4-2-5 under Corey Hetherman's former structure — disciplined, low-explosives.
Minnesota.dt: Perpetually top-30 in explosive-play prevention; pressure comes from scheme. They lose to teams that out-talent them, never to chaos.
s: The most stable identity in the West division footprint
Elite explosive-play prevention year over year
Fleck wins the games he should
w: Loses the line of scrimmage vs elite fronts

deep.intro: 2025: 8-5. SP+: #45, 6.0 wins. Fleck's Year 10 formula is unchanged: top-25 defense (five in six years projected), physical ball, and an offense that keeps the ceiling nailed shut — no top-50 unit since 2020, and 14 or fewer points in 22 of his 44 losses. QB Drake Lindsey returns for Year 2 with experience everywhere (Darius Taylor, three linemen, Cincinnati transfer Noah Jennings), but the numbers were grim (62.0 QBR, 9.7 yards per completion). The defense lost its best player to a conference rival — safety Koi Perich to Oregon, the pettiest transfer of the Big Ten cycle — but returns Anthony Smith (12.5 sacks), Maverick Baranowski, and John Nestor (six INTs). Prediction: 6-6, a bowl, and the same conversation next July. Fleck's seat: room temperature forever.

Nebraska.ob: Rhule's pro-spread — quick game and RPOs with Dylan Raiola, TJ Lateef pushing after his freshman flashes during Raiola's injury.
Nebraska.ot: Dylan Raiola left for Oregon in the spring portal — the room is now Anthony Colandrea (the UVA transfer senior) vs TJ Lateef, whose freshman flashes during the injury stretch earned a real shot. Skill room rebuilt through the portal again.
Nebraska.db: 3-3-5 pressure system — Rhule's rebuilt front seven finally has B1G bodies.
Nebraska.dt: Year-three Rhule defenses historically spike; 2025's close-game curse (one-score losses again) was offense-inflicted.
s: Rhule's year-three track record (Temple, Baylor both spiked)
QB room has two legitimate answers
w: Still has not beaten a top-15 team under Rhule

deep.intro:
2025: 7-6 — Year 3 stagnation instead of the classic Rhule Year 3 leap. SP+: #37, 6.0 avg wins, No. 12 schedule.

The gist. Dylan Raiola is gone; UNLV's Anthony Colandrea arrives — a scramble-heavy, big-play-hunting, occasionally self-destructive joyride (nine career multi-INT games) that Dana Holgorsen will point at WRs Nyziah Hunter and Jacory Barney Jr. The line adds ISU, LSU, and South Carolina transfers; sophomore RBs Mekhi Nelson and Isaiah Mozee replace 1,400-yard Emmett Johnson — with the July complication that Nelson was just arrested on an aggravated battery charge, status unresolved. Defense: Rob Aurich is the fourth coordinator in five years, importing his San Diego State fix-it kit plus SDSU players around promising sophomores (Williams Nwaneri, Kade Pietrzak). Prediction: 6-6 or 7-5 against the league's 12th-toughest schedule, and either way the Rhule conversation sharpens: two straight 7-6s reads as progress until it reads as ceiling. Warm-adjacent, trending on results.

Northwestern.ob: Braun-ball: outside zone, heavy formations, field-position chess with a new QB after Preston Stone's one-year rental.
Northwestern.ot: The new lakeside stadium opens with the same formula: shorten games, live in third-and-manageable, upset one ranked team a year.
Northwestern.db: 4-3 quarters, fundamentals-first — routinely better than its talent.
Northwestern.dt: Braun's defenses tackle and align; they lose to speed, not scheme. Explosives allowed spike only against top-15 offenses.
w: Zero explosive offense two years running

deep.intro:
2025: 7-6, best team in five years, top-30 defense and special teams. SP+: #49, 5.4 avg wins... with the No. 6 schedule in America.

The gist. The vision is delicious — Chip Kelly as OC, MSU transfer Aidan Chiles at QB, and a roster built on sheer mass (three transfer linemen averaging 6-7, 328; a 225-pound RB; a 275-pound TE; a Division II tight end sensation in Luke Dehnicke) — and the schedule is a war crime: road games at ALL THREE of Ohio State, Oregon, and Indiana, plus Penn State, Iowa, and Illinois at home. The new lakefront-replacing Ryan Field opens in October. Six of 15 defensive regulars return, all six good (Fussell, Fitzgerald, Turner headline). Prediction: 5-7 with one upset that breaks somebody's season — Connelly's betting the same. Braun's seat is cool; the athletic department just built him a stadium.

---

Purdue.ob: Barry Odom's year two — spread with QB run elements, still hunting an identity after a bottom-of-the-league 2025.
Purdue.ot: The portal churned the entire two-deep again. Whatever emerges, the schedule (B1G West-legacy draws) offers a winnable September.
Purdue.db: Odom's 3-3-5 pressure roots — the one unit that showed year-one progress.
Purdue.dt: Havoc rates climbed late in 2025; the back end remains the league's most targetable.
s: Odom rebuilt UNLV fast — the blueprint exists
Low bar: any bowl is a win
w: Talent gap vs the league median is wide

deep.intro:
2025: 2-10 (0-9). SP+: #83, 3.5 wins. Odom's Year 2 after the 54-transfer Year 1: a calmer 29-transfer reload around QB Ryan Browne, Texas RB Jerrick Gibson, and a defense with a few real Big Ten players (Ian Jeffries, Charles Correa, FIU do-everything nickel Mister Clark — a Connelly favorite). The program has a documented history of decade-long walkabouts, and Indiana's simultaneous ascension is salt in every wound. Prediction: 3-9, better on tape than in the standings, with the Bucket game as the season.

---

Rutgers.ob: Schiano's pro-style — power run, play-action, new QB after Kaliakmanis' three-year run ended.
Rutgers.ot: The Rutgers formula is fixed: win the kicking game, run it 55%, keep games under 50 snaps of stress. Athan's departure makes the pass game a projection.
Rutgers.db: 4-3 base with Schiano's DB pedigree — sound, physical, rarely shredded twice the same way.
Rutgers.dt: Quietly top-40 defense most years; the offense's inability to stretch fields keeps it on the field too long.
w: Pass offense among the B1G's worst
New QB with a thin WR room
Ceiling is bowl eligibility, not the CFP race

deep.intro: 2025: 5-7 with the best Schiano offense since 2007 — wasted by a defense that fell to 95th in SP+ (dead last nationally in yards per successful play), which is the single most un-Schiano sentence ever written. SP+: #61, 5.4 wins. The response: total defensive teardown — South Dakota's Travis Johansen as coordinator (his first FBS job), only TWO returning defenders with 100+ snaps, and a two-deep of G6/FCS disruptors. The offense keeps the fun: WR KJ Duff (maybe the league's second-best returning receiver), RB Antwan Raymond, and a QB derby between BC transfer Dylan Lonergan and sophomore AJ Surace. Prediction: 5-7. Schiano's equity is enormous, but an 18th-year legacy coach betting his identity on an FCS coordinator and 15 new defensive starters is the definition of a prove-it season.

UCLA.ob: Bob Chesney arrives from James Madison after the DeShaun Foster experiment failed — his JMU teams were fast-tempo, RPO-heavy overachievers.
UCLA.ot: Nico Iamaleava's status defined the offseason (draft-eligible after a turbulent two years) — verify the QB room before air. Chesney's system elevates mid-tier talent; Westwood gives him more.
UCLA.db: Chesney brings the JMU pressure identity — multiple fronts, havoc-hunting.
UCLA.dt: The 2025 defense quit late; a full culture rebuild is underway. G5-to-P4 coaching jumps hit defense first — expect year-one turbulence.
s: Chesney has won everywhere he's been — three straight successful builds
LA talent base underexploited for a decade
Tempo identity fits the roster's athletes
w: G5-to-B1G jump is the biggest in coaching
Program has churned three HCs in five years

deep.intro: 2025: 3-9 — an 0-4 start, Foster fired after three games, a shock three-game surge (ending the Franklin era at Penn State 42-37 and blowing out MSU), then five straight blowout losses and the program's worst SP+ finish since 1963. 2026: Bob Chesney arrives from James Madison with 132 career wins across three levels and half the Dukes' roster — RB Wayne Knight (1,770 scrimmage yards), DE Sahir West (16 TFLs), All-Sun Belt linemen Riley Robell and Carter Sweazie, and more. The headline retention: QB Nico Iamaleava stayed, alongside G Eugene Brooks and CB Rodrick Pleasant. SP+: #48, 6.0 wins. Connelly won't predict a Cignetti-style surge but openly likes the setup: proven production in nearly every unit, from a coach who has won everywhere. Prediction: 6-6 and bowling, with Nico's redemption arc the storyline — vibes can change fast, and this program just proved it in both directions within one season.

Wisconsin.ob: Fickell's survival pivot: run-first 12 personnel behind the league's most veteran OL — the Air Raid detour is dead.
Wisconsin.ot: Colton Joseph arrives from Old Dominion — a genuine dual-threat spark for the run-first pivot, not a game-manager. The late-2025 identity shift plus a QB with juice is the bounce-back case.
Wisconsin.db: Fickell's 3-3-5 remains legitimately good — the offense wasted it two years running.
Wisconsin.dt: Top-25 defensive metrics in back-to-back seasons with zero offensive support. If the run identity holds, the wins math flips.
s: Defense has been quietly elite through the chaos
OL continuity best in the West footprint
w: Joseph makes the G5-to-B1G jump — translation risk is real

deep.intro:
2025: 4-8, the worst season since 1990 — and then the school retained Fickell anyway in early November, with more NIL money, and watched the team upset Washington and Illinois immediately after. SP+: #62, 5.4 wins. The bet for Year 4: Old Dominion QB Colton Joseph (2,624 pass/1,100 rush/34 TDs), Connelly's second-favorite transfer in the country and a potential "program savior" — his words — behind a retooled backfield (Abu Sama III, Nate Palmer, Darrion Dupree) with a defense (Jung, Catalano, Cheeks, Posa) that has never been the problem. The problem: the offense has been 86th-or-worse in offensive SP+ for three straight years, and only one returning player caught more than 11 passes. The seat: publicly cooled, functionally warm — nobody, including Connelly, knows where the Year 5 bar sits, which is its own kind of pressure. Prediction: 6-6 and a bowl — Joseph is a genuine difference-maker, and 17-21 coaches who get public votes of confidence tend to get one real bounce. If it's 4-8 again, the NIL-instead-of-buyout decision becomes a case study.

---

Arizona.ob: Brent Brennan's spread resets around a new QB after Noah Fifita's four-year era ended.
Arizona.ot: 2025's rebound (bowl return) came from defense and a softer schedule; the offense turns over its top three passers and its identity with them.
Arizona.db: 4-2-5 with legitimate back-seven talent — the roster's strength.
Arizona.dt: Top-40 pass defense in 2025; the front six needs a portal infusion to hold the line vs the league's power runs.

deep.intro: 2025: 9-4 (6-3) — the boom half of a program that only does booms and busts (top-30 or outside the top-75 in SP+ seven straight years). SP+: #30, 7.6 avg wins. CBS: 8-4 (losses at BYU, at Tech, Utah, at K-State).
deep.roster: Noah Fifita enters Year 4 as the starter with a career line closing on legendary quiet: 9,183 yards, 73 TDs — Crawford's argument is that he's the most undersold player in the West, and his final campaign is the national coming-out. The offense around him retools: leading rusher and top two receivers gone, sophomore slot Gio Richardson and transfers Antwan Roberts (Marshall) and Cole Rusk (Illinois) step in, and four of seven line starters return with Washington and SJSU veteran adds. The defense is the spine — coordinator Danny Gonzales survived an injury-lottery 2025 (21 different starters) and returns eleven of them, led by edge Chase Kennedy, LB Taye Brown, and secondary playmakers Jay'Vion Cole and Gavin Hunter, reinforced by eleven transfers including USC's Matai Tagoa'i and Oregon's Daylen Austin. Field position is the hidden identity: the defense fed an inefficient offense great starting spots all year, and Brennan's fourth-down aggression cashed them.
deep.identity: Strengths: QB stability, defensive continuity, coaching rhythm in Year 3. Weaknesses: skill-corps proof, and the schedule — unlike the top three, Arizona plays EVERYBODY, opening the gauntlet at BYU on Sept. 12. Goals: survive the connectivity tax and play kingmaker; a 9-win repeat with this slate would be worth ten on anyone else's.
deep.prediction: 8-4 — with the caveat that which contenders they beat (BYU? Utah at home? K-State?) will decide the CCG field. Brennan's seat is cool and warming in the good direction.

Arizona State.ob: Dillingham's tempo-spread — Sam Leavitt's return from injury restores the league's best QB-coach chemistry outside Lubbock.
Arizona State.ot: Sam Leavitt left for LSU in the spring portal — a gut punch. The room is now veteran journeyman Mikey Keene vs Kentucky transfer Cutter Boley; Dillingham QB development is the bet, superstar continuity is gone.
Arizona State.dt: Havoc-rich and bust-prone; they need the offense scoring 30+ to let the gambles ride.
s: Dillingham develops QBs regardless of the name
Tempo stresses the league's thin two-deeps

deep.intro: 2025: 8-5 — an injury-shredded title defense salvaged by the Tech upset. SP+: #42, 6.0 wins. Dillingham's Year 4 is a full reset: 25 transfers in, only 11 of 34 regulars back, and a four-way QB derby (Kentucky's Cutter Boley the favorite over Mikey Keene, Cameron Dyer, and Jake Fette). The skill room rebuilds around 7.5-ypc backfield flashes (Kyson Brown, Jason Brown Jr.), Colorado's Omarion Miller, and Delaware State's Marquis Gillis; the line kept faith with returnees despite losing five of seven. Brian Ward's defense leans on C.J. Fite and transfers Emar'rion Winston and Ashton Stamps — with the fun wrinkle that ASU plays Kansas at WEMBLEY on Sept. 19 and visits Texas A&M (SEC-doc crossover) in nonconference. Prediction: 6-6 — the banked 2024 title buys Dillingham all the patience this rebuild needs, and Boley is a sneaky 2027 name.

Baylor.ob: Aranda survived and won the offseason: DJ Lagway arrives from Florida — the highest-rated transfer in program history running Jake Spavital's spread.
Baylor.ot: Sawyer Robertson's graduation plus Lagway's arrival makes this the league's most fascinating offense: elite arm talent meeting a proven system. Waco expectations just tripled.
Baylor.db: Aranda's multiple front — his personal play-calling stint continues; disguise-heavy, light boxes.
Baylor.dt: 2025 defense was middling; Aranda's own scheme brain remains the unit's best asset and its bottleneck (HC doing DC things).
s: Lagway is a program-changing talent get
Aranda's defensive mind with his seat finally cool
w: Aranda's game-management still costs a game a year

deep.intro: 2025: 5-7 despite a 3,681-yard QB season — the Bears lost EVERY game they didn't score 30, because the defense has averaged 92nd in SP+ for three straight years. SP+: #51, 5.9 wins (Vegas leans under at -108). The full treatment: Aranda is 36-37 in Year 7, 16-21 over three years, and Connelly flatly ranks the seat among the nation's hottest. The save-the-job plan is a spectacular double-bet: DJ Lagway at quarterback (the former No. 1 recruit, post-chaotic-portal-tour, with new OC Jake Spavital tasked with making the talent into a quarterback) and K-State's Joe Klanderman as the third DC hire (six K-State defenses averaging 37.7 in SP+, four Wildcats in tow including nickel Daniel Cobbs, plus Indiana champ DT Hosea Wheeler). The offense keeps almost nothing — three returnees with 200+ snaps — but the transfer starting XI has real talent. How he escapes: Lagway hits, the defense jumps 40 spots, 7-5 with a bowl — genuinely achievable, since eight of twelve games project within one score. How it ends: the close games break like they have for three years (Baylor's a favorite in only two of those eight coin flips), Lagway's turnover habit follows him, and Waco moves on in November. Prediction: 6-6 and a sweat — Lagway is the single highest-variance player in the conference, and this is the league's best weekly soap opera either way.

Cincinnati.ob: Satterfield's balanced spread — Brendan Sorsby's breakout 2025 made this a top-25 offense; his draft decision defined the offseason (verify the QB room).
Cincinnati.ot: If Sorsby's back, everything works: QB run, play-action seams, the league's most underrated OL. If not, it's a reset.
Cincinnati.db: 3-3-5 — improved but still the program's limiting factor.
Cincinnati.dt: Bottom-half in havoc and explosives allowed; they won 2025 shootouts by having the better QB.
s: Sorsby (if returning) is top-3 in the league

deep.intro: 2025: 7-6 — the third straight year of modest gains. SP+: #50, 5.9 wins. Satterfield's whole 2026 is a bet on mid-major arbitrage: Georgia Southern QB JC French IV likely starts, the league's most-decorated returning O-line trio (Cotton, Tengesdahl, Tyo) protects him, and the skill corps and secondary are stitched from the MAC, FCS, and Division II. The elephant: the Sorsby gambling probe's Cincinnati chapter — the school is publicly denying it knew about his betting, and that story will not stay quiet. The seat: lukewarm; incremental improvement has bought time, but Connelly's ceiling question is the honest one, and 5-7 with this line would end the patience. Prediction: 6-6, bowl on a knife's edge.

Colorado.ob: Prime's year four — Julian Lewis' offense now, with the five-star sophomore taking full ownership after splitting 2025.
Colorado.ot: The Shedeur/Hunter era's afterglow faded fast in 2025; Lewis is the reboot. Expect pro-style dropback with Shurmur-tree structure and a rebuilt OL — again.
Colorado.db: 4-2-5 — Livingston-era aggression with annual portal turnover.
Colorado.dt: Boulder's defense has been a new cast every September; talent flashes, cohesion lags. Year-four culture verdict comes due.
s: Prime's brand still wins portal battles
w: OL remains the program's chronic failure
Defense unproven for the third straight year

deep.intro:
2025: 3-9 (1-8) — the post-Shedeur/Hunter cliff, a rotating QB carousel that confused everything, and a season Deion Sanders spent managing serious health problems. SP+: #65, 4.6 wins — last in the league's projections and Vegas's win totals. The full treatment: this is Year 4, 16-21 overall, and a third-or-fourth total roster reset — an on-field case that would have any normal coach squarely on the hot seat. The counterweights are real too: the brand economics, the recruiting gravity, and a health situation that makes the whole conversation different in kind — handle it with care on air. The football case for optimism is Connelly's favorite contrarian take in the league: he likes almost everyone Deion added. New OC Brennan Marion (instant-improvement résumé), blue-chip redshirt freshman QB Julian Lewis, an outstanding transfer WR trio (Danny Scudero, Kam Perry, Texas's DeAndre Moore Jr.), seven veteran O-line adds, FBS tackling-machine LB Gideon Lampron (123 tackles, 18.5 TFLs), SEC-proven nickel Randon Fontenette, and a talent flier on Tennessee's exiled playmaker Boo Carter. Connelly's line: more proven production than even the nine-win 2024 roster had. How this goes right: Lewis-Marion clicks, 6-6, a bowl, and the program story flips overnight. How it goes wrong: the floor we saw last year, and the hardest conversation in the sport arrives. Prediction: 5-7, better than the record, with Lewis the reason to watch every week.

---

Houston.ob: Willie Fritz ball: run-first, clock-first, QB-run spread — the 2025 bowl return validated the rebuild's floor.
Houston.ot: The QB room remains committee-flavored; Fritz teams historically peak in year three, which is now.
Houston.db: 4-2-5, sound and physical — the roster's better half.
Houston.dt: Top-half Big 12 defense in 2025 on effort and structure; talent acquisition hasn't caught up to scheme yet.

deep.intro: 2025: 10-3 — Willie Fritz's Year 2 rocket, right on his career pattern (.507 in Year 1s, .730 in Year 2s, .725 in Year 3s). SP+: #36, 7.4 avg wins.
deep.roster: QB Conner Weigman returns off a 2,705-pass/795-rush/36-TD season, and Fritz reunited him with the Tulane band: RB Makhi Hughes (2,779 yards over two Tulane years before a lost Oregon detour) comes home to a Fritz-Nagle offense, Tulane guard Shadre Hurst and nickel Javion White follow, and the line adds all-conference transfers Drew Terrill and Anthony Boswell. Slot Amare Thomas and big-play sophomore Koby Young return; Oregon State's leading receiver Trent Walker joins. The insurance policy is outrageous: five-star freshman Keisean Henderson, a top-five national recruit, backs up Weigman. The defense returns its secondary core (Webb, Allen, James) and front-six vets (Mack, Fotu, Laufau) with an Oregon/Okie State/Yale tackle infusion.
deep.identity: Strengths: possibly the league's best combined trench play, QB continuity, a coach whose Year 3s are historically monsters. Weaknesses: the schedule (at Tech Week 3, at K-State, at Utah — all three contenders on the road), secondary depth post-McCutchin. Goals: crash the CCG conversation and make the Henderson succession a 2027 luxury, not a 2026 necessity.
deep.prediction: 8-4 with the Week 3 trip to Lubbock as the league's first great litmus test — cover the "Houston is the sleeper" take now, because if they steal that one, this becomes the race everyone wanted. Fritz at 66 is playing with house money and knows it.

Iowa State.ob: Jimmy Rogers (via Washington State, before that 15-0 at South Dakota State) replaces Campbell — expect his physical, TE-heavy, defense-first program model.
Iowa State.ot: Rocco Becht's graduation plus the HC change makes the offense a full reset; Rogers' FCS-dynasty pedigree says structure arrives before stars.
Iowa State.db: Rogers is a defensive builder — the Cyclone 3-3-3 tradition should evolve, not vanish.
Iowa State.dt: Campbell left the cupboard solid but the stars left with him (transfers followed him to PSU). Grade this roster in October.
s: Rogers won everywhere, including a natty run at SDSU
Big 12 middle is soft enough to stay bowl-bound
w: Campbell took momentum and some players east
First P4 HC job — the jump is real

deep.intro: 2025: 8-4 — then Campbell left for Penn State and FIFTY-FIVE players entered the portal, about half following him east. 2026: Jimmy Rogers (27-3 and an FCS title at South Dakota State, then a one-year Washington State improvement job) inherits the most complete teardown in the Power 4: no returning offensive player with more than 87 scrimmage yards, no returning passing yards, five returning defenders with 100+ snaps. The rebuild: QB Jaylen Raynor (8,694 career yards at Arkansas State), Tulane's 3.0-yards-per-route WR Omari Hayes, and a defense stocked with twelve former Cougars under coordinator Jesse Bobbit. SP+: #64, 5.3 wins; CBS says 4-8. Prediction: 5-7, with the defense respectable fast and the offense a year away. Rogers wins everywhere eventually; "eventually" is the operative word.

Kansas.ob: Leipold's post-Daniels era begins — the veteran spread stays, the six-year QB finally graduated.
Kansas.ot: Leipold's system has produced top-40 offenses with three different QBs; the OL and TE room make the floor. The new starter inherits structure, not chaos.
Kansas.dt: The defense keeps Kansas in games it has no business winning; explosives allowed spike vs elite speed.
s: Leipold is the league's best pure program coach
w: Talent ceiling honest: 8 wins is the peak

deep.intro: 2025: 5-7, again — nine losses in eleven one-score games over two years, the cruelest stretch in the league. SP+: #55, 5.8 wins. Jalon Daniels is finally out of eligibility, and the offense hands itself to a derby (Cole Ballard vs. Isaiah Marshall vs. Rice's Chase Jenkins) with one massive tailwind: OC Andy Kotelnicki is BACK in Lawrence after his Penn State detour — KU had a top-30 offense in each of his last two years. The transfer skill haul (K-State's Dylan Edwards — a rivalry poach and elite returner — Syracuse's Yasin Willis, Buffalo's Nik McMillan) is fun; the defense returns solid pieces (Harris, Herold, Lathan) under second-year DC D.K. McDonald. The frame: Leipold is explicitly hoping to pull a Matt Campbell — Iowa State's 12-losses-in-13-one-score-games stretch flipped into 10 wins in 11 and an 11-win season. Prediction: 6-6 and a bowl as the luck normalizes. The frustration is real, but 173 career wins and the Kotelnicki return buy the year.

Kansas State.ob: Klieman's spread-option hybrid around Avery Johnson's final act — the league's most schemed QB-run offense.
Kansas State.ot: Johnson's decision to return (verify) sets the ceiling; the RB-by-committee and elite special teams do the rest. 2025 underachieved the talent.
Kansas State.db: 4-3 quarters, disciplined — the Wildcat formula since Snyder.
Kansas State.dt: Rarely elite, never bad: 15 straight years of top-60 defense. Wins come from +1.5 turnover margin game scripts.
s: Best special teams in the league
Klieman never has a lost season
w: Thin margins vs the league's new money teams

deep.intro: 2025: 6-6 — a preseason top-18 team that lost five of seven one-score games and never fixed the run game; Chris Klieman retired. 2026 coach: Collin Klein, first year — the program-legend QB and former OC, back after two years running Texas A&M's offense. Connelly's word for the hire: it just seems perfect (with the "so did Frost-to-Nebraska" asterisk attached). SP+: #29, 8.2 avg wins. Vegas: ~+1200 tier.
deep.roster: QB Avery Johnson stayed — the whole ballgame. His 2025 was an identity crisis (elite athlete intermittently cosplaying as a pocket passer), and Klein, the human-third-down-conversion himself, is the ideal whisperer. Around him: RBs Joe Jackson and Oklahoma State transfer Rodney Fields Jr., returning targets Jaron Tibbs and TE Garrett Oakley, plus blue-chip WR transfers Josh Manning (Missouri) and Izaiah Williams (A&M). The line is the fear: All-conference LT John Pastore and then six transfers and a juco. The defense, handed to A&M co-coordinator Jordan Peterson, was gutted (six of 19 regulars back) but restocked with Connelly's favorite front-six haul in the league: edges Wendell Gregory (15.5 TFLs on a 1-11 Oklahoma State team) and Elijah Hill, LB Mekhi Mason (15 TFLs at Louisiana Tech), plus secondary vets Zashon Rich, Donovan McIntosh, Koy Beasley, and Ja'son Prevard. Watch list: Johnson's decision-making, Gregory, the O-line.
deep.identity: Offense: Klein's QB-run-forward physicality, tailored to Johnson. Defense: speed-and-havoc front, question-mark depth. Strengths: QB ceiling, the schedule jackpot (misses Tech, BYU, AND Utah — projected favorite in eleven games, underdog only at TCU), no Dublin curse this year (Connelly's joke, but the 2025 Week 0 Ireland loss really did poison the season). Weaknesses: portal-built lines on both sides, first-year coordinators everywhere. Season goals: 9+ wins and the CCG — the path is genuinely open.
deep.prediction: 9-3 with a real shot at Arlington on tiebreakers and scheduling alone — the classic Big 12 "back into the title game, then shock someone on Friday night" candidate. If Johnson plays instinctively, this is the league's best dark horse. Klein's seat: a honeymoon with generational goodwill.

Oklahoma State.ob: Eric Morris (from North Texas, Air-Raid tree) inherits the post-Gundy rebuild — tempo and spacing arrive in Stillwater.
Oklahoma State.ot: Eric Morris brought his QB with him: Drew Mestemaker followed from North Texas — instant system continuity in a total rebuild. The 2025 teardown still defines the roster around them.
Oklahoma State.db: Full defensive rebuild — 2025 was the league's worst unit by most measures.
Oklahoma State.dt: There is nowhere to go but up: bottom-10 nationally in EPA allowed. Morris' UNT defenses were opportunistic, not stout.
w: Worst P4 roster inherited this cycle
The FPI residual (-12.3) was real, not noise

deep.intro: 2025: 1-11 (0-9) — the worst Cowboys team in 60-plus years, the end of the Gundy era. 2026 coach: Eric Morris, first year — imported from North Texas with roughly half of the NATION'S NO. 1 OFFENSE. SP+: #39, 6.4 avg wins — Connelly calls it aggressive and admits he can't stop thinking they'll be really good anyway.
deep.roster: The single most fascinating roster flip in the country: QB Drew Mestemaker (4,300 yards at UNT), RB Caleb Hawkins (1,400), WRs Wyatt Young (1,200) and Miles Coleman, and two all-conference linemen (Braydon Nelson, Johnny Dickson III) all followed Morris to Stillwater — plus explosive adds Tre Page III (7.5 ypc at Tarleton), Chris Barnes (Wake Forest — another ACC-doc crossover name), and Israel Polk. Connelly will be shocked if this isn't a top-20 offense. The defense decides everything: coordinator Skyler Cassity (the Sam Houston miracle-worker who made UNT competent) brings six Mean Green starters, UCLA LB Isaiah Chisom, and a stack of DII/backup fliers. Average defense = bowl and beyond; bad defense = fun 6-6.
deep.identity: Morris joins the Deion '23 / Cignetti '24 / Belichick '25 lineage of new-coach-mass-transfer projection nightmares — and Connelly's track record on those calls is good, which makes his open bullishness here notable. Seven conference games projected within a touchdown means the win total could swing wildly either way.
deep.prediction: 7-5 and the league's feel-good story — the offense travels, the defense is average enough, and improving on 1-11 by six games earns Morris coach-of-the-year votes he won't win. Watch the Oregon nonconference visit (from the Ducks' CBS win list) for a measuring-stick beatdown, then judge from October on.

TCU.ob: Dykes' Air Raid descendant — Josh Hoover's 3,900-yard 2025 makes his draft decision the program's fulcrum (verify).
TCU.ot: With Hoover: top-15 passing offense, four-wide spacing, tempo bursts. Without: Dykes has recruited well enough to reload, not repeat.
TCU.db: 3-3-5 — improved to league-average after two leaky years.
TCU.dt: Gillespie's unit finally stopped the bleeding in 2025; run defense on standard downs remains the soft spot.
s: Hoover (if back) is the league's best pure passer
Dykes wins the ones he should
w: Fort Worth NIL trails the league's arms race

deep.intro: 2025: 9-4 — two straight 9-4s, but tight-win dependent and 37th in SP+. SP+: #34, 7.1 avg wins. Sonny Dykes Year 5.
deep.roster: The league's most fun experiment: Josh Hoover left for Indiana, and Dykes replaced him with HARVARD's Jaden Craig — 6-3, 230, 48 TDs against 10 picks over two Ivy seasons — running a new offense under UConn import Gordon Sammis, whose Huskies threw ONE interception all of last year. RB Jeremy Payne broke out late (476 yards over the final five), the receiver room mixes Jordan Dwyer, Ed Small, and zero-drop transfer Jeremy Scott, and LT Ben Taylor-Whitfield anchors a retooled line. The defense's 2025 split was comical — 19th against the run, 109th per dropback, no pass rush whatsoever — but six of seven linemen return, Paul Oyewale is the star-turn candidate, and transfers Cheta Ofili (from Tech!) and Koron Hayward bring elite pressure rates. Three transfer DBs rebuild the back.
deep.identity: Strengths: coaching, offensive scheme upgrade, four soft openers (opponents all 58th-or-worse) for Craig's acclimation. Weaknesses: pass rush proof, secondary chemistry, and a NOVEMBER FROM HELL — four straight against the league's top five, including hosting BYU Oct. 3 and Utah Nov. 21. The trap: TCU opens against North Carolina in Dublin — and Connelly's beloved Dublin Loser Curse has wrecked the loser of that game every year it's existed. (For your ACC crossover segment: Belichick's UNC is on the other side. Someone's season starts cursed.)
deep.prediction: 8-4 — Craig is going to work, the November gauntlet is going to take two, and Dykes will quietly finish top-30 again while everyone argues about whether he's underrated. His seat: cool, with a "the title game was four years ago" hum underneath.

UCF.ob: Scott Frost's year two — tempo-option spread, the 2017 nostalgia hire now needing 2026 results.
UCF.ot: Year one was ugly (bottom-half Big 12); the system needs a dual-threat trigger the portal was tasked to find. Tempo without efficiency is just fast punting.
UCF.dt: Orlando talent keeps the athletes flowing; structure and two-deep depth lag. Explosives allowed were a league-worst issue.
s: Frost's system has a proven UCF ceiling
w: Year one showed no offensive identity

deep.intro:
2025: 5-7 — a near-exact replica of Frost's 2016 first-tour season, which he followed with 13-0, a symmetry he'd very much like to keep. SP+: #60, 6.0 wins. The 2026 case: JMU's Alonza Barnett III (5,404 pass / 1,398 rush / 71 TDs over two years — instantly one of the league's most proven QBs and a Connelly favorite transfer) pairs with Louisville's boom-or-boom RB Duke Watson behind an experienced-if-imported line, while Alex Grinch's defense returns a legitimately fantastic senior corner trio (Bellamy, Jackson, Bell). Prediction: 7-5 — the biggest year-over-year jump in the league outside Stillwater, and the Barnett-Watson highlight package becomes appointment viewing. No, not 13-0. Probably.

---

West Virginia.ob: Rich Rodriguez year two — the QB-run spread's originator still installing his triple-threat backfield vision.
West Virginia.ot: 2025 was a 6-win transition with flashes; the portal brought his kind of QB (verify the winner). Pace + QB run + vertical RPOs is the endgame.
West Virginia.db: 3-3-5 stack (Rodriguez tradition) — pressure from depth, gambles behind.
West Virginia.dt: Casteel-tree structure returning; 2025 was leaky vs power runs. Morgantown defenses round into form in odd years, history says.
s: Rodriguez's system fits the roster he's building

deep.intro: 2025: 4-8 in Rich Rod's return — the transfer class Connelly loved mostly didn't click. SP+: #66, 5.2 wins. Round two: a QB battle between flash-showing sophomore Scotty Fox Jr. and Oklahoma blue-chip transfer Michael Hawkins Jr., feeding Jacksonville State mega-back Cam Cook (1,659 yards — a Connelly top-10 transfer) behind two all-conference Jax State linemen. The defense is a near-total do-over (TWO returning 200-snap players) under Zac Alley, patched with G6 disruptors and eight jucos. Prediction: 5-7, more watchable, still thin — and Connelly, burned once, is conspicuously not predicting overachievement this time. Rich Rod's seat: Year 2 grace, Year 3 questions loading.

Boston College.ob: Bill O'Brien's pro-style — TE-centric, protection-first, NFL install running on a college clock.
Boston College.ot: The QB room stays unsettled after two years of rotation; O'Brien's system rewards whoever protects the ball. Run game leans gap-scheme behind a veteran interior.
Boston College.dt: They align right and tackle; havoc is bottom-quartile ACC and pass rush is a committee. Games stay close on structure alone.

deep.intro: Coach: Bill O'Brien, Year 3 (9-16). Hot seat: top-10 nationally (Athlon #7-range).
deep.roster: The response to a lost season was drastic: O'Brien fired the structure — he's now his own offensive coordinator, with 62-year-old Ted Roof running the defense. The transfer class is genuinely intriguing for this level: RB Evan Dickens (Liberty) rushed for 1,339 yards — ninth nationally despite 11 games — with 3.8 yards after contact per carry; Connelly called him an outright steal. QB is the wild card: Mason McKenzie, a dual-threat jumping all the way from Division II Saginaw Valley State, had an up-and-down spring. The line gets G6 starters Reggie Jackson (Jacksonville State) and Owen Snively (Eastern Michigan); Colgate WR Reed Swanson and TE Kaelan Chudzinski round out the weapons. Defense adds LB Anthony Palano (Washington State), Harvard edge Alex DeGrieck, and ex-blue-chips Kris Jones (Georgia) and KJ Sampson (FSU) up front — but the returning secondary helped BC rank 126th in yards per dropback, and Roof hasn't run a strong defense since the 2010s.
deep.identity: The plan is transparent — physical, Dickens-powered, clock-controlling offense to protect a D2 quarterback and a bad defense. Strengths: Dickens, tight end, special teams (28th). Weaknesses: nearly everything else — pass protection, pass defense, and depth. Goal: competence. Non-conference trips to Cincinnati and Rutgers will tell you everything by mid-September.
deep.prediction:
CBS's most brutal projection in the league: 1-11, 0-8 — a winless ACC slate. SP+ is gentler (4.0 wins) but 74th overall.

4) The seat: How he got here: the 4-1 start of 2024 created belief; BC is 5-15 since, with the offense imploding via sacks/turnovers and a defense that stopped doing anything well. Two years in with regression, at a program that can't out-spend its mistakes. How he escapes: the identity has to work immediately — feed Dickens 25 times a game, shorten games, steal the Rutgers/Cincinnati tossups, and scrap to 4-5 wins with visible fight. Anything resembling that CBS projection ends the era; even Saturday Down South framed it plainly — below .500 brings the real-estate agents.

California.ob: Tosh Lupoi (from Oregon DC) takes over post-Wilcox, keeping sophomore Jaron-Keawe Sagapolutele — the ACC's best young lefty — as the centerpiece.
California.ot: Sagapolutele's freshman 3,000-yard flash is the program's asset; expect a modern spread built around his rhythm game (OC hire defines the details — verify).
California.db: Lupoi's specialty: movement fronts, deep DL rotation, recruiting-driven talent upgrades.
California.dt: The Wilcox-era discipline stays in the walls; Lupoi's Oregon fronts were faster than anything Cal fielded. Transition friction is the only question.
s: Sagapolutele is a two-year franchise QB
Lupoi recruits at a level Cal hasn't seen
w: Skill room needs two portal hits

deep.intro: Coach: Tosh Lupoi, Year 1. New hire, clean slate.
deep.roster: Justin Wilcox was let go after nine years of aggressively .500 football (13-13 across the Mendoza and Sagapolutele eras; 62-68 over 11 years), and Cal handed the keys to Lupoi — elite recruiter, former Cal DL, fresh off four years running Oregon's defense. His first and biggest win: keeping QB Jaron-Keawe Sagapolutele, the blue-chip sophomore who threw for nearly 3,500 yards as a true freshman. Then he built around him with the ACC's second-best portal class (#14 nationally, 30 commits): RBs Adam Mohammed (Washington), Carter Vargas (Cal Poly), Ashten Emory (UTEP); WRs Chase Hendricks (Ohio), Ian Strong (Rutgers), Cooper Perry (Oregon); OL Jimothy Lewis Jr. (Miss State) and Ashton Rivera (Kent State) joining four returning linemen with 32 combined starts. The defense — which lost star corners Hezekiah Masses and Brent Austin — got a youth infusion of ex-blue-chips (Oregon's Jericho Johnson, Kamar Mothudi, and Kingston Lopa; A&M's Solomon Williams; Tennessee's Emmanuel Okoye) plus vets like Georgia CB Daniel Harris and Arizona CB Marquis Groves-Killebrew.
deep.identity: TBD, and that's the intrigue: 30-year-old OC Jordan Somerville arrives from the Buccaneers preaching pro-style (Connelly is openly allergic to that phrase), and DC Michael Hutchings inherits talent that needs assembly. Strengths: the QB, O-line continuity, upside everywhere. Weaknesses: front-line defensive proof, chemistry, first-year-staff variance. Goal: bowl comfortably, develop JKS into a star, and make the Calgorithm mainstream.
deep.prediction: CBS calls them a dark-horse ACC sleeper at 7-5 (5-4) — they don't play Miami — while SP+ hedges at 6.1 wins. The road slate (UNLV, SMU, NC State, Virginia) decides whether this is cute or real.

Clemson.ob: Dabo's pro-spread — 2025 was the program's worst season in 15 years, and the offense resets around a new QB after Klubnik.
Clemson.ot: Garrett Riley's system needs a distributor; the freshman class has the pedigree. The bigger question is philosophical: Dabo finally used the portal — barely.
Clemson.db: Tom Allen's aggressive 4-2-5 — the one 2025 unit that kept its standard.
Clemson.dt: Front-seven talent is still blue-chip; the 2025 collapse was offense and vibes. Explosive allowance stayed top-25.
s: Blue-chip roster ratio still top-5 nationally
A wounded Dabo has responded before
w: Portal resistance is now a proven handicap
Program aura of inevitability is gone

deep.intro: Coach: Dabo Swinney, Year 18 (186-53). Not on the hot seat — but on a hot-seat list ("others receiving votes" per Athlon) for the first time in his career. That's the story.
deep.roster: A genuine reset. Gone: QB Cade Klubnik, three top-50 NFL draft picks off the defense, star OT Blake Miller, five of the top seven defensive linemen, and seven offensive starters overall. The offense belongs to junior Christopher Vizzina — a two-year backup who looked solid in his one career start — with true freshman Tait Reynolds lurking. Skill talent is real if healthy: WRs Bryant Wesco Jr. (injured most of '25) and T.J. Moore, RB Gideon Davidson plus SMU transfer Chris Johnson Jr. Five of the nine linemen who started last year (injuries everywhere) are back. Dabo finally leaned into the portal — 10 transfers, a career high though still below the national average — headlined by edge London Merritt (Colorado), DT Markus Strong (Oklahoma), and safeties Corey Myrick (Southern Miss) and Jerome Carter (Old Dominion). Players to watch: DE Will Heldt (17.5 TFLs — a legit All-America candidate) and the LB duo of Sammy Brown and Kobe McCloud, maybe the ACC's best.
deep.identity: Here's the spicy part — the offense is going backward to go forward: Chad Morris returns as OC, the man who modernized Clemson from 2011-14 but hasn't coordinated a good offense since 2017. Skeptics see a regressive hire; believers see a culture fit who can build a clean, QB-friendly system around Vizzina. Defensively they've been less than the sum of their parts for three straight years (outside the SP+ top 20 each season despite blue-chip talent). Strengths: front-seven star power, home-heavy early schedule (five of the first seven at home). Weaknesses: unproven QB, rebuilt secondary, big-play allowance, and the psychological residue of 2025. Goal: a redemption arc — get back to Charlotte and quiet the "is the dynasty over" chorus.
deep.prediction:
Fascinating split — SP+ ranks Clemson 23rd, second-best in the ACC, while Vegas lumps them at 7.5 wins with the middle class and CBS says 8-4 (6-2) with losses to LSU, Miami, Virginia Tech, and South Carolina. They're the league's hinge team: the Oct. 3 Miami visit and Oct. 31 trip to FSU will decide whether this is a bounce-back or a slow burn.

4) The seat, such as it is: How he got here: three straight years of defensive underachievement, a 2025 collapse from #4 to 7-6 (worst since 2010), and a stubborn late arrival to portal-era roster building. How he gets out: it doesn't take a title — a 9-or-10-win season with a competent Vizzina and a top-15 defense resets the entire narrative, and the schedule gives him a 5-2-or-better runway. What keeps him safe regardless: the résumé, the buyout, and an administration with no appetite for a divorce.

Duke.ob: Manny Diaz's reigning ACC CHAMPIONS — Darian Mensah's title-winning offense now needs a new arm after his Miami transfer.
Duke.ot: The system (tempo spread, layered play-action) returns intact with the OC; the QB who ran it doesn't. Duke's roster is the deepest it's been in program history.
Duke.db: Diaz's pressure-forward 4-2-5 — top-20 havoc, turnover manufacturing.
Duke.dt: The championship formula was defense + Mensah; the defense half returns. They'll blitz on 3rd-and-4 with a lead in the fourth. Fear nothing.
s: Reigning ACC champs with the DC-turned-HC formula working
Program money and momentum at all-time highs
w: Title defense with a target on every week

deep.intro: Coach: Manny Diaz, Year 3 (18-9). Safe — two 9-win seasons and a trophy will do that.
deep.roster: No champion in America got robbed harder this offseason. Miami took Darian Mensah AND Cooper Barkate in a late-January portal raid (the Mensah NIL dispute got legal before settling), and the bleeding didn't stop there — WR Que'Sean Brown left for Virginia Tech and Terry Moore for Ohio State. The rebuild: QB Walker Eget (San Jose State), a veteran who somehow threw for 3,000 yards on a torn ACL last season; WRs Jared Richardson (Penn, 1,000-yard FCS producer) and Javen Nicholas (Charlotte, 700 yards); TE Nate Kurisky (Louisville). The good news is homegrown: RB Nate Sheppard ran for 1,100 yards as a freshman and gets portal reinforcements CJ Campbell Jr. (Rutgers/FAU) and Wilhelm Daal (Yale), behind two returning line starters plus tackles Nick Del Grande (Coastal, All-Sun Belt) and Braden Miller (Cal). Defensively Diaz mostly trusted his own development pipeline in the front seven (jr DT Preston Watson, blue-chip soph edge Bryce Davis, returning LB Luke Mergott) while adding secondary help: Che Ojarikre (Stanford), Dylan Flowers (WKU), Patrick Smith-Young (North Texas).
deep.identity: Diaz's whole tenure has been "one elite unit at a time" — 2025's 19th-ranked offense with an 86th-ranked defense, after the reverse in 2024. The offense will regress without Mensah/Barkate but shouldn't collapse; the defense has room to climb back toward Diaz's havoc-heavy standard. Strengths: coaching, backfield, improving secondary. Weaknesses: pass-game proof, rebuilt DL, and a nasty non-conference (Illinois, Tulane). Goal: defend the title with a straight face — and win the revenge game when Miami visits Durham, the pettiest date on the ACC calendar.
deep.prediction: CBS says 6-6 (4-5); SP+ says 6.5 wins but notes six projected one-score ACC games — Duke overachieved projections by 12+ points five times last year, so writing them off is how you get burned twice. Coach status: cold.

Florida State.ob: Norvell's tempo-spread survived the 2024 disaster with a 2025 rebound — year seven runs through a veteran portal QB room.
Florida State.ot: Malzahn-influenced run game (yes, that hire) with Norvell's RPO structure; identity is more physical than the CFP-era teams.
Florida State.db: Multiple 4-2-5 rebuilt via portal for the third time.
Florida State.dt: 2025 was league-average with flashes; the talent is sufficient, cohesion decides. Tallahassee's boom-bust cycle continues.
w: Program whiplash: CFP to 2-10 to reset in 36 months

deep.intro: Coach: Mike Norvell, Year 7 (38-34). CBS hot-seat rating: 5.0 — unanimous, the hottest seat in college football (shared only with Wisconsin's Fickell and Baylor's Aranda).
deep.roster: Tommy Castellanos is gone after the classic Castellanos arc (torched projections by 17 points a game through September, faded hard after). New QB: Ashton Daniels (Auburn, ex-Stanford) — steadier passer, less explosive, physical runner. The skill core is genuinely good: RB Ousmane Kromah (breakout candidate) plus Texas transfer Quintrevion Wisner, and WRs Duce Robinson and Micahi Danzy both rank among the ACC's top six returning receivers in yards per route. The terrifying part: the top seven offensive linemen are all gone — five transfers arrived (All-MAC T Nate Pabst, Auburn's Xavier Chaplin) and up to four could start. Defense: LB duo Blake Nichelson + Southern Miss tackle-machine Chris Jones should make the run D nasty; DTs Daniel Lyons and Jordan Sanders push the pocket; but sophomore Mandrell Desir is the only proven pass rusher and the secondary lost four of its top six (including All-ACC S Earl Little Jr.). Portal class ranked 26th nationally / 5th in the ACC — and notably, the biggest "wins" were retentions of young talent.
deep.identity: New wrinkle — Gus Malzahn retired in February, receivers coach Tim Harris Jr. was promoted to OC, and Norvell is taking a heavier hand in his own offense. Expect his up-tempo, RPO-heavy system with more QB run. Tony White's 3-3-5 improved in bend-don't-break fashion (54th in success rate allowed, but 6th in yards allowed per successful play). Strengths: skill talent, linebackers, run defense. Weaknesses: a fully transfer-built O-line (the single most common failure mode in college football), pass rush, secondary depth, and a psyche that's absorbed two years of losing. Goals: honestly? Survive. No road win since 2023 — get one. Get bowl-eligible early, then steal one of the big ones.
deep.prediction:
SP+ actually respects the roster (35th, 6.6 wins) but the schedule is a meat grinder — SMU, Alabama, Louisville, and Miami all by mid-October, Clemson on Halloween, Florida to close. CBS projects 6-6 (4-4) with losses to every good team on the slate.

4) The seat — how he got here and how he escapes: The arc is brutal: 13-0 in 2023 → the CFP snub → 2-10 in 2024 → a 5-7 "rebound" in 2025 that still meant 7-17 across two years with zero ACC relevance. FSU openly circled the Lane Kiffin drama in November and reportedly would have moved if it materialized; the $50M+ buyout is widely described as the only reason he's employed. Multiple outlets call 2026 make-or-break, some flatly a foregone conclusion that it's his last year. His path off: (1) the transfer O-line must gel immediately, because everything else follows from it; (2) Daniels has to be the stabilizer Castellanos wasn't in November; (3) beat one of Alabama/Miami/Clemson/Florida to change the emotional math; (4) win road games, plural. Realistically, 8-4 with a signature scalp saves him; 6-6 with losses to every ranked team means Tallahassee is hiring in December.

Georgia Tech.ob: Key's option-flavored spread lost Haynes King but kept the identity: QB run game, heavy motion, fourth-down conviction.
Georgia Tech.ot: King's toughness defined the 10-win 2025 near-CFP season. The new QB inherits the league's best-schemed run game and its most creative situational calls.
Georgia Tech.db: 3-3-5 — improved yearly under Key, still the lesser half.
Georgia Tech.dt: They stay in structure and force long drives; elite speed still stresses the perimeter. Progress is real but incremental.
s: Key's schematic identity is the ACC's most distinct
w: Defense a year behind the offense

deep.intro: Coach: Brent Key, Year 5 (27-20). Secure after three straight winning seasons.
deep.roster: The Haynes King era is over (7,907 passing / 2,277 rushing yards as a Jacket — a human third-down conversion), and the turnover is extreme: only 3 of 16 offensive players with 200+ snaps return (RB Malachi Hosley, OTs Ethan Mackenny and Malachi Carney), and the defense lost DT Jordan van den Berg plus five of its top six DBs. Both coordinators left too (OC Buster Faulkner to Florida, DC Blake Gideon to Texas). The reload is fun, though: QB Alberto Mendoza (Indiana — Fernando's little brother, who posted a 99.4 QBR in 102 garbage-time snaps: 18-of-24, 286 yards, 5 TDs, plus 190 rushing yards on 13 carries) and RB Justice Haynes (Michigan — 857 yards at 7.1 a pop and 10 TDs in just seven games). Key's portal tells you his priorities: six defensive linemen and two corners, including UConn's Vincent Carroll-Jackson, Colorado's Tawfiq Thomas, and ex-Bama blue-chips Noah Carter (edge) and Jaylen Mbakwe (CB). Watch: LB E.J. Lightsey is the one proven defensive anchor; 6-4 Elon WR Isaiah Fuhrmann needs to get healthy because the pass-catching cupboard is nearly bare.
