deep.identity: Line-of-scrimmage football — Key is an O-lineman to his core — now with an NFL flavor: new OC George Godsey (a GT alum back after 15 NFL seasons) and new DC Jason Semore (Marshall/Southern Miss). Expect a Haynes/Hosley-powered run game with play-action shots and a defense betting on unlocked athleticism. Strengths: backfield, tackles, elite special teams (11th in SP+). Weaknesses: receiver room, secondary rebuild, proven defensive bodies generally. Goal: keep the winning-season streak alive in an obvious reset year and set up a legitimate 2027 run.
deep.prediction: CBS 7-5 (5-3) feels right; SP+ is harsher (43rd, 6.1) mostly on schedule (Tennessee, at Georgia, Louisville, at Clemson, at Virginia Tech). A fourth straight winning season here would be quietly excellent coaching. Coach status: cold seat, warm buzz.

Louisville.ob: Brohm's QB-whisperer spread — a new arm (post-Miller Moss) in the most pass-friendly structure in the league.
Louisville.ot: Brohm's offenses finish top-25 regardless of the trigger man; the vertical timing game and TE seams stay. RB room is the sneaky strength.
Louisville.dt: Top-20 havoc, bottom-40 explosives allowed: the Brohm-era tradeoff in one line. They'll win a shootout and lose a coverage bust.
w: Coverage busts at the worst times

deep.intro: Coach: Jeff Brohm, Year 4 (28-12). Secure — three straight 9+ win seasons, but the "what-if" file is thick.
deep.roster: The Brohm formula continues: 30+ transfers, new QB, stable 9-win product. QB Miller Moss is gone; former Ohio State backup Lincoln Kienholz takes over (Davin Wydner of West Georgia behind him). The one elite returning unit: RBs Isaac Brown and Keyjuan Brown, arguably the country's best duo — 1,588 combined yards at 8.1 per carry with zero QB run threat helping them. The receiver room is fully rebuilt: Tre Richardson (Vanderbilt — big-play threat and dangerous kick returner), Lawayne McCoy (FSU), Jackson Voth (Drake), TEs Brody Foley (Tulsa) and Justyn Reid (Tulane). O-line: only guard Lance Robinson returns; five transfers arrive, led by All-CUSA T Anwar O'Neal (Delaware) and All-Sun Belt T Johnnie Brown III (Georgia Southern). Defense returns real stars — edge Clev Lubin (8.5 sacks), LB Stanquan Clark, nickel Antonio Watts (8.5 TFL), CB Tayon Holloway — but only 5 of 16 players with 200+ snaps, so 14 defensive transfers (Iowa S Koen Entringer, Tennessee S Kaleb Beasley, Kentucky CB DJ Waller Jr., Purdue DT Demeco Kennedy, UNC edge Tyler Thompson among them) fill the gaps under new co-DCs Steve Ellis and Mark Ivey. Portal class ranked ~18th nationally.
deep.identity: Brohm's aggressive, QB-friendly pass game — though with this RB room and an unproven QB, expect a more ground-forward tilt early. Strengths: backfield, edge rush, Brohm's track record of making portal rosters click. Weaknesses: Kienholz is the least-proven QB Brohm has started here, the OL is transfer-dependent, and the margins are razor thin — they've lost by combined single digits in the games that kept them out of Charlotte two straight years (three 2025 ACC losses by a combined 7 points). Goal: stop losing coin-flips and finally reach the title game.
deep.prediction: CBS has 9-3 with a monster 8-1 ACC mark; SP+ notes they're projected favorites in every game after the Ole Miss opener (a Sunday game in Nashville) but play eight projected one-score games. They don't face Miami, Clemson, or Virginia, and get SMU and Pitt at home. If Kienholz is good, they're a title-game team. Coach status: safe, and per CBS one of the sport's most underrated coaches.

NC State.ob: Doeren's spread behind CJ Bailey's year three — the ACC's most experienced returning QB nobody talks about.
NC State.ot: Bailey's steady rise (14:4 ratio in 2025's back half) plus a veteran OL makes this the quiet dark horse. Identity: efficiency over explosion.
NC State.db: 3-3-3 hybrid — Doeren's defensive roots keep the floor.
NC State.dt: Middle-of-the-pack everything in 2025 with youth everywhere; the two-deep returns more production than any ACC defense.
s: Bailey is a top-5 returning ACC QB
Most returning production in the league
w: Special teams cost them two 2025 games

deep.intro: Coach: Dave Doeren, Year 14 (95-70). Warm — not listed, but the Year 14 ceiling conversation is live.
deep.roster: Start here: CJ Bailey is the best returning QB in the ACC by Total QBR and is already climbing 2027 draft boards. Around him, though, it's a gut renovation — top rusher, top four pass catchers (the leading WR transferred to USC), and three OL starters all gone. Doeren signed eight skill transfers, headlined by WRs Victor Snow (Buffalo) and Joshisa Trader (Miami) — yes, a Canes-to-Pack storyline. The line adds an All-AAC gem in T Jimarion McCrimon (ECU — one sack allowed all year; Connelly called proven OL transfers "gold" and this one of the class's best) plus 6-8, 345-pound FCS mauler Jai'Lun Hampton. Defense: the pass rush was nonexistent, so edge Harvey Dyson (Tulane — 8 sacks for the American champs) and 336-pound DT Katron Evans (Marshall) arrive, with PSU S King Mack, UNC DB Ty White, and Georgia DB Ondre Evans joining a secondary that returns six 200-snap players, mostly sophomores/juniors.
deep.identity: Bailey's improvisation — a scrambler who rarely takes sacks — behind a huge line, attached to a defense that's ranked outside the SP+ top 60 two straight years (the program's historical identity, inverted). Strengths: QB, O-line size, young DB continuity. Weaknesses: pass rush until proven, brand-new receiver room, and a front-loaded schedule: Virginia in Brazil (Week 0), Vanderbilt, and Louisville all by Oct. 3 — though all three break in new QBs. Goal: turn the late-2025 surge (4-4 → 8-5) into a 9-win year and shut down the ceiling talk.
deep.prediction: SP+ 6.9 wins, Vegas 7.5, CBS a deflating 6-6 (4-5). The range of outcomes is enormous because Bailey alone is worth two wins. Coach status: another .500 season "would inevitably spark tougher questions" — CBS's words, and Raleigh's mood.

North Carolina.ob: Belichick year two — the NFL operating system (12 personnel, situational mastery) with a roster now fully his.
North Carolina.ot: Year one was a soap opera with a losing record; the football itself improved late. The QB room went full portal; the system asks for competence, not stardom.
North Carolina.dt: The defense tackled and aligned better than any recent UNC unit; it just couldn't rush P4 passers. Year two roster is bigger everywhere.
s: Late-2025 tape showed real structural progress
w: FPI's -12.1 residual said the market undersold the mess

deep.intro: Coach: Bill Belichick, Year 2 (4-8). Hot seat: 4.1 CBS rating, Athlon top-10.
deep.roster: Year 1's fatal flaw was a transfer class that Belichick and GM Michael Lombardi badly overrated (the "33rd NFL team" quote became a national punchline), leaving UNC 124th in offensive SP+. The Year 2 rebuild looks more coherent — though returning production is dead last in the ACC at 44%. QB: Billy Edwards Jr. (productive at Maryland, lost most of 2025 to injury at Wisconsin) — debatable ceiling, much higher floor. RB Demon June and slot Jordan Shipp are real returning pieces; the line adds size in JacQawn McRoy (Arkansas) and Andrew Threatt (Charleston Southern); and in peak Belichick fashion, he signed three transfer tight ends and a Patriot League star WR (Lehigh's Mason Humphrey). Defense was the 2025 bright spot under Steve Belichick (59th SP+, disciplined bend-don't-break): edge Melkart Abou Jaoude (10.5 sacks) headlines, with Leroy Jackson, Isaiah Johnson, and nickel Kaleb Cost back, plus a Richmond pipeline (edge Donovan Hoilette Jr., LB Peyton Seelmann).
deep.identity: The real hire is OC Bobby Petrino — whatever else you think of him, he has demonstrably improved recent offenses at Texas A&M and Arkansas, and pairing his scheme with Belichick's situational defense is at minimum fascinating television. Strengths: defensive discipline, coaching IQ in one-score games, a competent-or-better QB for the first time in the era. Weaknesses: zero proven OL, thin receiver talent, roster churn, and the noise machine. Goal per CBS: bowl eligibility = tangible, honest progress.
deep.prediction:
SP+ 58th, 5.3 wins; CBS 6-6 (4-4). The schedule guarantees main-character status immediately: TCU in Ireland (Week 0), at Clemson (Week 3), Notre Dame (Week 5). Win one of those and it's "Belichick redemption" headlines; start 1-3 and it's retirement-watch content by October.

4) The seat: How he got here: the most hyped hire in college football history producing a team that didn't beat a single FBS opponent with a winning record, wrapped in a year of off-field soap opera (the exit rumors he had to call "categorically false," the Lombardi discourse, the general circus). How he escapes: let Petrino run the offense without interference, ride the defense, win the winnable middle of the schedule, and — bluntly — keep the program out of the tabloids. Six or seven wins with visible discipline probably buys Year 3. Another 4-8 and both sides likely negotiate the exit everyone half-expects.

Pittsburgh.ob: Narduzzi's pro-spread around Eli Holstein's third year — the QB flashes have never yet sustained.
Pittsburgh.ot: Mason Heintschel — the true freshman who seized the job mid-2025 — is the guy now (Holstein moved on). His fearless intermediate game fits Kade Bell tempo; sophomore consolidation is the theme.
Pittsburgh.db: Narduzzi's 4-3 press-quarters — the longest-running defensive identity in the ACC.
Pittsburgh.dt: Still top-30 in havoc, still gambling on corners' islands, still occasionally torched deep. You know exactly what this is.
s: Heintschel is a rising sophomore starter with real tape
w: The ceiling has been 8-4 for a decade

deep.intro: Coach: Pat Narduzzi, Year 12 (80-61). Lukewarm — no formal danger, but patience isn't infinite.
deep.roster: The crown jewel returns: Mason Heintschel, who took over as a true freshman and threw for 2,354 yards and 16 TDs in 10 games (9 starts). Both lines are loaded — five of the top seven OL are back plus All-MAC G Keylen Davis (Akron), and the DL returns ends Isaiah Neal and Jimmy Scott with DT Nick James. The exodus is out wide and in the back: six of the top seven receivers gone, five of the top eight DBs gone, and star LB Rasheem Biles left for Texas. Adds: RB La'Vell Wright (WKU) to pair with sophomore Ja'Kyrian Turner, FCS star WR Malik Knight (Western Carolina), TEs Elijah Lagg (UAB) and Carson Kent (Oklahoma), nickel DeMarco Ward (Memphis), CB Raion Strader. Watch: WR Cataurus Hicks is the lone proven pass catcher; young DBs Shadarian Harrison, Shawn Lee Jr., Cruce Brookins, and Josh Guerrier all got baptism-by-injury reps last year.
deep.identity: Vintage Narduzzi — a top-5-nationally run defense (25.8% stuff rate, 3.6 yards per carry allowed) attached to a leaky pass defense (82nd in completion rate allowed), now paired with a legitimately modern QB. 2025 Pitt was the platonic borderline top-40 team: 7-1 against everyone outside the SP+ top 40, 1-4 against it. Strengths: Heintschel, both lines, run D. Weaknesses: receiver proof, secondary rebuild, and a schedule with road trips to Virginia Tech, Miami, Louisville, and Cal. Goal: turn Heintschel Year 2 into the top-20 breakthrough this program keeps flirting with.
deep.prediction: The widest spread in the league: Vegas 7.5, SP+ 6.9 — and CBS at a pessimistic 6-6 (3-6) with a possible four-game skid to close and zero signature wins. If the young DBs grow up fast, the over hits easily; if not, it's a grumpy November on Cardiac Hill.

Stanford.ob: Tavita Pritchard returns to the Farm as HC (via the NFL) after the GM-era interim years — pro-style, QB-development-first.
Stanford.ot: Andrew Luck's GM structure now has its permanent coach. The roster is years from ACC-competitive; the identity (smart, physical, patient) is at least clear now.
Stanford.dt: Bottom-quartile ACC in most metrics; the two-deep is the league's thinnest. Progress = competitive Novembers, not bowls yet.
s: Luck's GM model brings NFL rigor
w: Multi-year rebuild, year one of it

deep.intro: Coach: Tavita Pritchard, Year 1. New hire — the seat question is years away.
deep.roster: The full context: Stanford has been the worst power-conference program of the 2020s — 7-39 against teams ranked better than 80th over five years, average SP+ ranking of 102.8, no winning full season since 2018. GM Andrew Luck doubled down on the family tree: Pritchard (former Stanford QB and longtime assistant, most recently Commanders QB coach) as HC, former Stanford OL coach Terry Heffernan as OC, with ex-Seahawks DC Kris Richard the one outside hire. The roster has individual bright spots — RB Micah Ford (strong after contact), G Simione Pale (back from injury; excellent in 2024), tackling machine LB Matt Rose (a stop every 6.1 snaps), and CB Brandon Nicholson, one of the ACC's best. But the most proven receiver is a Yale transfer (Nico Brown), the DL replaces four of six, and QB is 24-year-old Michigan transfer Davis Warren — a respected leader with a career line of 5.4 yards per dropback and 7 TDs to 10 INTs.
deep.identity: The stated dream is restoring the Harbaugh/Shaw power-run DNA — the problem being Stanford ranked 132nd at 3.9 yards per non-sack carry last year, so the identity currently exists only in the brochure. Strengths: linebacker/secondary experience, culture continuity, house money. Weaknesses: line play on both sides, QB ceiling, skill talent, kicking game (128th ST). Goal: be competitive, win the games you should (Hawaii and Elon are must-haves), and start recruiting like the plan is real.
deep.prediction: CBS 2-10 (0-9); SP+ 3.9 wins vs. a 3.5 total. This is a foundation-pouring year, full stop. Coach status: safe by definition.

Syracuse.ob: Fran Brown's physical spread — the QB room resets again after the Angeli year, with the run game now the identity.
Syracuse.ot: 2025 regressed hard once injuries hit; Brown's recruiting (top-25 classes) hasn't matured yet. Expect run-first ball control while it does.
Syracuse.db: 4-2-5 with Brown's DB-guru fingerprints — young and fast.
Syracuse.dt: The -12.1 FPI residual flagged them as market-overrated entering 2025, and the season proved it. The talent influx is real; 2026 is its first test.

deep.intro: Coach: Fran Brown, Year 3 (13-12). Warm — the 10-2 debut equity is real but depleted.
deep.roster: The season died in September: Steve Angeli (4 solid starts) went down against Clemson, and Syracuse averaged 11.1 points the rest of the way while losing eight straight by an average of 27.5. The fix is a genuine three-man QB derby — Angeli is back but Brown has pointedly not named him the starter, with dual-threat Amari Odom (Kennesaw State) and former five-star Malachi Nelson (UTEP) brought in to push him. Around them: RB Ahmad Miller (Jackson State), sized-up WRs Cole Weaver (Miami-OH), Elijah Moore (FSU), and Matt Outten (PSU), TE Noah Meyers (WKU), and an O-line returning five players with starting experience averaging 6-6, 331 — mostly underclassmen. The defensive bet is coordinator Vince Kehres (Toledo), whose 2025 unit ranked 21st in SP+ and smothered the pass. He inherits aggressive corners Demetres Samuel Jr. and Chris Peal and imports pass rush: FCS All-American Keyshawn Johnson (UT Martin, 13.5 sacks), Tunmise Adeleye (UNLV), and 300-pound Jartavius Flounoy (Georgia State).
deep.identity: Brown's brand is swagger, recruiting, and toughness; Kehres brings structure to a defense that has regressed 20+ SP+ spots three straight years — Connelly is confident that streak ends even if a leap doesn't come. Strengths: secondary, young OL beef, chip-on-shoulder energy. Weaknesses: QB uncertainty, skill-position proof, and the ACC's new nine-game gauntlet. Goal: stabilize, pick a QB early, and avoid a second straight lost October.
deep.prediction: CBS 3-9 (1-8); SP+ 4.5 wins; total 4.5. Coach status: no formal danger, but a second straight bottom-three finish would turn up the temperature fast in Year 4.

Virginia.ob: Elliott's balanced spread coming off the 10-win breakthrough — but Chandler Morris' graduation empties the trigger seat.
Virginia.ot: The 2025 formula (veteran portal QB + turnover margin) worked once; replicating it is the test. OL returns four.
Virginia.db: 4-3 multiple — opportunistic, sound, senior-laden in 2025 and younger now.
Virginia.dt: The turnover luck (+14) will regress; the structure underneath was legitimately improved. Expect honest mid-tier.
w: Morris' void + turnover-luck regression = double drag

deep.intro: Coach: Tony Elliott, Year 5 (22-26). Fully off the hot seat — last year flipped his tenure.
deep.roster: The 2025 miracle was a portal Hail Mary that landed — and now they have to do it again. Only 16 of the 40 players who logged 200+ snaps return, so Elliott signed 30 more transfers (Connelly ranked it among his 20 favorite classes). QB Chandler Morris (3,000 yards) is gone; Beau Pribula (Missouri, ex-Penn State) takes over. The additions skew explosive-on-bad-teams: RBs Jekail Middlebrook (MTSU) and Solomon Beebe (UAB), WRs Rico Flores Jr. (UCLA) and Da'Shawn Martin (Kent State), plus Connelly's favorite big man in the class, DT Zion Wilson (ECU — 9.5 TFLs, 7 sacks), and ten defenders with FBS starts. The returning core is legit: third-team All-ACC linemen Noah Josey and McKale Boley front what's billed as the most experienced O-line in the country, plus DE Fisher Camac, CBs Jam Jackson and Donavon Platt, and LB Kam Robinson. Sustainability note: only 14 of the 30 transfers are seniors — a first attempt at stability.
deep.identity: Ball control + a defense that jumped from 86th to 19th in SP+. Strengths: O-line, secondary experience, and motivation — the OT title-game loss to Duke cost them a playoff berth and they have not forgotten. Weaknesses: total dependence on portal chemistry for the second straight year, and a QB downgrade until proven otherwise. Goal: prove 2025 was a program change, not a lightning strike, and get back to Charlotte.
deep.prediction: The nerds and the humans disagree: SP+ has them 40th with regression baked in; CBS projects 7-5 (4-5). But there's not a single projected top-30 opponent on the schedule, so if this transfer class hits at even 80% of last year's rate, 9+ wins is live. Week 0 vs. NC State in Brazil is a wild opener. Coach status: ice cold.

Virginia Tech.ob: James Franklin in Blacksburg — the shock hire of the cycle brings his pro-style program machine to a fanbase starving for relevance.
Virginia Tech.ot: Franklin's floor (24 winning seasons of infrastructure discipline) meets a roster Pry left thin. Expect a QB-run-friendly pro-spread and instant recruiting jumps.
Virginia Tech.dt: The 2025 unit was bottom-half ACC; Franklin hires proven coordinators and the lines improve first, historically.
s: The ACC's middle is very winnable

deep.intro: Coach: James Franklin, Year 1. Obviously safe — this is the league's most anticipated debut.
deep.roster: The weirdest stat in the conference: a program that just fired its coach ranks 4th nationally in returning production (69%). Most of a very young O-line returns, plus DT Kemari Copeland, LB Kaleb Spencer, and scrappy corners Isaiah Brown-Murray and Thomas Williams. Franklin then imported State College wholesale: QB Ethan Grunkemeyer (85.2 QBR and 76% completions over his last three PSU starts), TE Luke Reynolds, and OC Ty Howle all followed him, and the recruiting class is loaded with flipped Penn State commits. The portal haul (ESPN's #14 class) is deep: CBs Jaquez White (Troy — 3 INTs, 12 PBUs, Connelly's pick as the class's biggest immediate-impact guy) and Cam Chadwick (UConn), young ex-blue-chip edges Javion Hilson (Missouri) and Mylachi Williams (PSU), RB Bill Davis (Louisiana), WRs Que'Sean Brown (Duke) and Marlion Jackson (La. Tech). Watch: RB Marcellous Hawkins and Davis are both elite yards-after-contact runners.
deep.identity: Penn State South — efficient, physical, TE-friendly, run-anchored, and yes, occasionally conservative. The twist of the year: Brent Pry, the fired head coach, stayed on as Franklin's defensive coordinator. Freed from CEO duties, Pry gets to just coach a unit that underachieved last year. Strengths: continuity nobody expected, a semi-proven QB, a soft on-ramp (no projected top-30 opponent until Oct. 24 at Clemson). Weaknesses: receiver proof, and the usual Year 1 alignment wobbles. Goal: 7-8 wins, restore the identity, and let the blue-chip recruiting wave land for 2027.
deep.prediction: CBS says 8-4 (5-4); SP+ says 7.3 wins against a 6.5 Vegas total. The Oct. 24 Clemson trip and the season-ending Commonwealth Clash vs. Virginia are the measuring sticks. An 8-win debut makes Franklin the story of the ACC's middle class.

Wake Forest.ob: Jake Dickert's year two — the slow-mesh RPO tradition is gone; his identity is tempo-run and quarters defense.
Wake Forest.ot: Year one overachieved to bowl eligibility on discipline; the offense remains the ACC's least explosive by design and necessity both.
Wake Forest.db: 4-2-5 quarters — Dickert's specialty and the roster's better half.
Wake Forest.dt: Top-half ACC in success-rate defense immediately; the line of scrimmage still loses to the league's upper third.
w: Least explosive offense in the league

deep.intro: Coach: Jake Dickert, Year 2 (9-4). Extremely secure — arguably the best pure coaching job in the ACC last year.
deep.roster: Last year's 9-4 came from a defense built of G6/FCS transfers that ranked 9th nationally in both success rate and yards per play, plus a 4-1 record in one-score games — with a negative turnover margin. Now the offense turns over: RB Demond Claiborne is gone, four OL starters left, and the QB job goes to Gio Lopez — the former South Alabama star and one-year North Carolina bust, betting Dickert can restore him. Skill adds are classic Wake value shopping: RBs Sawyer Seidl (North Dakota) and KD Daniels (Florida), WRs Wondame Davis Jr. (UTEP), Ny Carr (Miami), and Chase Tyler (Duke), around returning star WR Carlos Hernandez. The defense keeps its teeth: edges Langston Hardy and Gabe Kirschke (23 TFLs, 12 sacks combined), elite nickel Davaughn Patterson, nine of 17 returning 200-snap players, plus corners Deuce Blades (FIU) and Bernard Causey III (Georgia State) and FCS finds Matt Herron (DT, Weber State) and Vincent Firenze (LB, Dayton).
deep.identity: Tough-as-nails, defense-first, margin-hunting football — the anti-analytics team that keeps cashing anyway. Strengths: edge rush, secondary scheme, situational coaching. Weaknesses: an all-or-nothing offense (four games of 16 or fewer points last year) now breaking in a reclamation QB behind a rebuilt line. Goal: prove 2025 wasn't a one-score-game mirage.
deep.prediction: The market and the model both fade them hard — CBS projects 4-8 (2-7), largely because the schedule is arguably the ACC's cruelest (road: Louisville, NC State, Cal, SMU, Georgia Tech; home: Miami and Duke). SP+ is kinder at 6.2 wins. Given what Dickert did with less, the under-the-radar bowl push is live. Coach status: ice cold.

Oregon State.ob: JaMarcus Shephard (via Alabama co-OC) takes over the rebuilt-Pac-12 flagship — spread with WR-room pedigree.
Oregon State.ot: The two-year independence wilderness ends with a real conference; the roster churned annually and the QB room is open. Shephard's energy is the program bet.
Oregon State.db: 4-2-5 reset — Beaver defenses stayed respectable through the chaos.
Oregon State.dt: Corvallis keeps producing NFL DBs; the front needs restocking. The new Pac-12 schedule is winnable immediately.

Washington State.ob: Kirby Moore (from Missouri OC) inherits the Rogers-to-Ames fallout — his Drinkwitz-tree spread meets a roster on its third HC in three years.
Washington State.ot: Moore's Missouri offenses were motion-heavy and RB-led; Wazzu's roster fits the skill spots better than the lines. Rebuilt Pac-12 slate offers a fair fight.
Washington State.db: 3-3-5 tradition continues — speed over size, pressure over patience.
Washington State.dt: The two-deep survived the poaching better than 2024's version; structure will beat talent in this league's middle.
s: The new Pac-12 is winnable year one
w: Third HC in three years — churn tax

Army.ob: Monken's flexbone triple option — the purest run identity in FBS.
Army.ot: Post-Daily QB transition; the machine reloads inside. Clock control caps possessions near 8 a game.
Army.db: 3-4 bend-don't-break sized for option-week prep opponents.
Army.dt: Assignment-sound, undersized; they win by keeping your offense in street clothes.
w: Talent gaps show vs P4 speed

Charlotte.ob: Tim Albin's veer-and-shoot influence — rebuilt after a bleak-everything 2025.
Charlotte.ot: Year two of the Albin rebuild; QB from the portal. The offense showed late-season signs; the roster is still bottom-quartile American.
Charlotte.dt: Bottom-tier havoc and explosives allowed; progress is measured in competitive quarters.

East Carolina.ob: Houston's balanced spread — steady, veteran-driven, quietly 8-win capable.
East Carolina.ot: Katz-era QB stability ended; the run game and OL return the league's sneakiest floor.
East Carolina.dt: Sound vs the run, targetable deep; they keep games in the 20s.
s: Home crowd best in the league

Florida Atlantic.ob: Zach Kittley's Air Raid — the league's pace-and-volume passing lab.
Florida Atlantic.ot: Year two: the system produced yards immediately; points and wins need a defense. QB competition is open but the plug-in history is proven.
Florida Atlantic.dt: If it climbs to merely bad, the offense makes them a bowl team.

Memphis.ob: Charles Huff (from Southern Miss' turnaround) inherits a perennial 10-win machine.
Memphis.ot: Silverfield left a stocked roster; Huff's Marshall/USM teams ran harder than Memphis' recent identity — expect balance shift.
Memphis.dt: League-average unit with above-average takeaway timing; good enough behind that offense.

Navy.ob: Newberry's flexbone-with-RPO evolution — more pass than Army, same soul.
Navy.ot: Horvath's graduation hands it to the next trained pilot; the 2025 offense was top-20 rushing efficiency.
Navy.dt: The Army-Navy formula: erase explosives, force patience your opponent doesn't have.
s: Option efficiency with a real RPO layer
w: QB transition in a specialist system

North Texas.ob: Neal Brown returns to the G5 — inheriting the tempo machine Eric Morris built and took to Stillwater.
North Texas.ot: Drew Mestemaker followed Eric Morris to Oklahoma State — brutal double exit. Tayven Jackson (the veteran Indiana transfer) headlines the retooled room; Neal Brown pragmatism replaces tempo maximalism.
North Texas.db: 3-3-5 — the 2025 weak half of a great team.
North Texas.dt: Bottom-third league defense rode shotgun on a top-10 G5 offense; Brown's rebuild focus starts here.
s: Roster at program peak outside the QB room
Brown is a proven G5 program CEO
w: Lost the HC and the QB in one offseason

Rice.ob: Scott Abell's option-spread — the Davidson architect's second FBS year.
Rice.ot: 2025 overachieved to bowl eligibility on scheme surprise; year two loses the novelty edge.
Rice.dt: League-bottom talent playing league-median defense — coaching margin personified.

Temple.ob: K.C. Keeler's veteran program-building — year two off a surprise bowl.
Temple.ot: The FCS-legend playbook: transfers with chips, run-first pragmatism, special teams wins.
Temple.db: 4-2-5 — improved a full tier in one Keeler year.
Temple.dt: Structure over stars; November fades when depth runs out.

Tulane.ob: Will Hall promoted to keep the machine running after Sumrall's Florida exit.
Tulane.ot: The roster made back-to-back league title games; Hall's own USM offenses were RPO-heavy and RB-led. QB room resets again — it always does, and it always reloads.
Tulane.db: 4-2-5 — the league's most talented defense two years running.
Tulane.dt: Sumrall's structure remains in the walls; retention through the transition looked strong.

Tulsa.ob: Tre Lamb's year two — tempo spread with QB run, rebuilding from the league's basement.
Tulsa.ot: Year one showed fight and little else; the portal brought a new QB room.
Tulsa.dt: Bottom-quartile most metrics; progress is competitive Novembers.

UAB.ob: Alex Mortensen (Saban-tree, promoted from interim) — pass-first spread revival.
UAB.ot: The interim stretch showed a real offensive identity; full offseason install plus Birmingham's portal appeal is the bet.
UAB.dt: Nowhere to go but up from bottom-five nationally; even median defense means bowls.

UTSA.ob: Traylor's physical spread — RPO glance plus the hardest-playing culture in the league.
UTSA.ot: Post-Owens QB retool; the identity (third-down conversion machine, TE-friendly) is coach-proof by now.
UTSA.db: 4-2-5 veteran structure — always sound, occasionally special.
UTSA.dt: Top-half league unit annually; loses only the talent battles.
s: Traylor's culture is G5 gold standard
w: P4 poaching bleeds the two-deep yearly

Delaware.ob: Carty's balanced pro-spread — year two of FBS transition went bowl-eligible; the climb is real.
Delaware.ot: QB stability carried 2025; the jump to full CUSA rounds meets a thin two-deep.
Delaware.dt: Structure travels; line-of-scrimmage depth doesn't yet.
s: Culture from the FCS title runs

Florida International.ob: Willie Simmons' spread — the FAMU winner's first FBS build, year two.
Florida International.ot: Miami-area portal finds keep the skill room competitive; the lines lag a class behind.
Florida International.dt: Speed shows in flashes; sustaining four quarters is the project.
s: Simmons won big at every stop

Jacksonville State.ob: Run-heavy spread — the post-Rodriguez identity carried by a strong OL room.
Jacksonville State.ot: Back-to-back title-game trips prove the roster model; QB is a portal rental annually. Verify the current staff before air.
Jacksonville State.dt: Top-third league havoc; deep balls hurt them when the gambles miss.

Kennesaw State.ob: Bohannon's option-influenced ground game — the +16.4 FPI residual outlier of 2025.
Kennesaw State.ot: The market undersold them all season (our model flagged it); the triple-option DNA now blends portal spread elements.
Kennesaw State.dt: They tackle like an FCS-champion program because they were one.
s: Proved the doubters wrong once already

Liberty.ob: Chadwell's option-spread — QB-run identity hunting a return to the NY6 level.
Liberty.ot: 2025 disappointed (-7.9 FPI residual said the market overrated them); the QB room and OL retool.
Liberty.dt: League-average with big-play leaks; the offense must carry again.

Louisiana Tech.ob: Cumbie's Air Raid roots — pass-volume identity with a defense that finally showed up in 2025.
Louisiana Tech.ot: Bowl return built on the league's surprise defense; the offense now must match it.
Louisiana Tech.dt: Top-quartile league unit; regression risk with graduations.

Middle Tennessee.ob: Derek Mason's physical rebuild — defense-first culture in year two.
Middle Tennessee.ot: The offense remains the project; run-game commitment rose all 2025.
Middle Tennessee.dt: Improved a tier in year one; talent still bottom-half league.

Missouri State.ob: Casey Woods (from SMU OC) — FBS year two with a tempo-balanced attack.
Missouri State.ot: Transition roster overachieved in 2025; Woods brings P4 structure to the install.
Missouri State.dt: League-bottom talent, league-median effort; the gap closes slowly.

New Mexico State.ob: Tony Sanchez's veteran-laden spread — perennial scrappy underdog.
New Mexico State.ot: QB churn again; the identity is physical run and special teams.
New Mexico State.dt: Competitive floor established; ceilings require upsets.

Sam Houston.ob: Phil Longo's Air Raid — pass-first volume meets a program built on defense.
Sam Houston.ot: The -16.4 FPI residual (most underrated by market in our 2025 model) reflected a roster better than its brand. Longo's install year two should score.
Sam Houston.dt: Top-half league takeaways; size is the limiter.

UTEP.ob: Scotty Walden's tempo spread — the youngest HC energy in the league, year two.
UTEP.ot: Offense jumped a tier in 2025; the El Paso portal sell is culture and playing time.
UTEP.dt: Bottom-third league; effort present, depth absent.

Western Kentucky.ob: Helton-tree Air Raid tradition — pass-volume identity with annual portal QBs.
Western Kentucky.ot: The formula never changes: 300 yards passing, bowl eligibility, coordinator poaching. Verify HC continuity before air.
w: Ceiling capped at league title game

Akron.ob: Moorhead's RPO spread — year four of the slow climb.
Akron.ot: Offense finally showed his Penn State-era fingerprints in stretches.
Akron.dt: Bottom-third MAC; close-game record decides bowl math.

Ball State.ob: Mike Uremovich's power-spread rebuild, year two.
Ball State.ot: Ground-first identity taking hold; QB unsettled.
Ball State.dt: Improvement from awful to below-average — trajectory matters.

Bowling Green.ob: Eddie George's pro-style — the celebrity hire proved substance in year one.
Bowling Green.ot: Run-first, physical, TE-heavy; the locker room buys in fully.
Bowling Green.dt: League-median with a real identity; that's progress.

Buffalo.ob: Pete Lembo's special-teams-and-structure program — quietly excellent 2025.
Buffalo.ot: Efficient run game, low turnovers, field position mastery.
Buffalo.dt: Top-third MAC; they beat you with fewer mistakes.

Central Michigan.ob: Matt Drinkall's smashmouth build — Army-tree toughness, year two.
Central Michigan.ot: Run-heavy, huddle-up, clock-control; the MAC's most deliberate offense.
Central Michigan.dt: Improved on effort; personnel still catching up.

Eastern Michigan.ob: Creighton's veteran program — perennial 6-win bowl machine.
Eastern Michigan.ot: Portal QB again; the formula is competence plus timing.
Eastern Michigan.dt: Median MAC; turnover margin is the whole story.

Kent State.ob: Mark Carney (promoted) inherits the league's longest rebuild.
Kent State.ot: Roster still recovering from the Burns-era teardown; wins are found money.
Kent State.dt: Bottom of FBS metrics two years running.

Massachusetts.ob: Joe Harasymiak's return to MAC play — defense-first rebuild, year two.

Massachusetts.dt: The defense is honest; the offense is the abyss.

Miami (OH).ob: Chuck Martin's veteran pro-spread — the MAC's steadiest program.
Miami (OH).ot: Post-Gabbert QB transition; OL and TE play carry the identity.
Miami (OH).dt: Structure and tackling; they lose only to speed.

Northern Illinois.ob: Hammock's power-run identity — MW-bound program playing its last MAC-style year? (verify alignment).
Northern Illinois.ot: RB-led, physical, low-variance; the 2024 Notre Dame upset aura faded into a rebuilding 2025.

Ohio.ob: John Hauser promoted after Brian Smith's single season — continuity for the MAC's best recent program.

Toledo.ob: Mike Jacobs (from D-II power Mercer... verify origin) inherits the MAC's most talented roster post-Candle.

Western Michigan.ob: Lance Taylor's balanced pro-spread — steady build, year four.
Western Michigan.ot: Run-game identity with a veteran OL; bowl-level baseline established.
Western Michigan.dt: Median MAC; special teams above average.

Air Force.ob: Calhoun's flexbone — four decades of option devotion.
Air Force.ot: The cycle repeats: new QB, same machine, 300 rushing yards.
Air Force.dt: Erase explosives, steal possessions; the formula is eternal.

Boise State.ob: Danielson's pro-spread — post-Jeanty era found its footing in 2025.
Boise State.ot: Madsen's graduation opens a heated QB room; the RB tradition never sleeps.
Boise State.db: 4-2-5 fast — the G5's most talented defense.
Boise State.dt: Top-25-caliber front; the blue turf standard holds.

Fresno State.ob: Matt Entz's physical program — FCS-dynasty structure in year two.
Fresno State.ot: Run-first identity, QB competition; the Valley talent base delivers.

Hawai'i.ob: Timmy Chang's run-and-shoot revival — Micah Alejado's breakout made it real.
Hawai'i.ot: The island's system is back: four-wide, choice routes, volume. Alejado is the G5's most fun watch.
Hawai'i.dt: They'll trade scores; the defense needs three stops, not ten.

Nevada.ob: Jeff Choate's toughness rebuild — year three of the climb.
Nevada.ot: Run-heavy, defense-first; wins inch upward annually.
Nevada.dt: Respectable now; explosive-thin offenses keep games ugly by design.

New Mexico.ob: Jason Eck's option-spread — the 2025 breakout (+15.1 FPI residual, market never caught up).
New Mexico.ot: Our model flagged them as the year's most underrated G5 team and the season validated it; the QB-run game returns its core.

San Diego State.ob: Sean Lewis' tempo spread — year two of pace in a program built on defense.
San Diego State.ot: The offense modernized; the QB room remains a competition.
San Diego State.dt: Top-third MW annually; the Aztec defensive standard survives coordinators.

San José State.ob: Ken Niumatalolo's option-influenced evolution — pass-heavier than his Navy roots, year three.
San José State.ot: Balanced attack around a veteran OL; the Spartans hover at bowl-line annually.

UNLV.ob: Dan Mullen's spread — year two, the Vegas project has real money and real expectations now.
UNLV.ot: Mullen's QB development resume meets a portal-built roster; 2025 delivered again and the bar is now league titles.
UNLV.dt: Havoc-rich, bust-prone; they out-athlete the league's middle.

Utah State.ob: Bronco Mendenhall's program discipline — year two of the culture transplant.
Utah State.ot: Run-first pragmatism, QB competition, penalty-free football.
Utah State.dt: Median MW, trending sound; the rebuild is ahead of schedule.

Wyoming.ob: Jay Sawvel's defense-first program — Laramie football as always.
Wyoming.ot: Ground game and play-action; the altitude and physicality do the rest.
Wyoming.dt: Top-third MW defense; offense's job is not to lose.

App State.ob: Dowell Loggains' pro-spread — the Boone standard rebuilding after two mid years.
App State.ot: NFL-tree QB coaching with a physical run base; the roster returns solid production.
App State.dt: Median Sun Belt now; the standard is higher and everyone in Boone knows it.

Arkansas State.ob: Butch Jones' veteran build — quietly back to bowl-level.
Arkansas State.dt: Competent; wins the games it should.

Coastal Carolina.ob: Ryan Beard (from Missouri State's rise) — young HC energy for the post-Chadwell drift.
Coastal Carolina.ot: The spread-option DNA remains in the roster; Beard's task is re-sharpening an identity that dulled.
Coastal Carolina.dt: The -8.8 residual flagged 2025's market overrating; honest reset year.

Georgia Southern.ob: Clay Helton's pro-style — steady bowl-level program.
Georgia Southern.ot: Veteran QB play annually; the option ghosts are long gone.
Georgia Southern.dt: Middle of the league both sides; special teams above.

Georgia State.ob: Dell McGee's ground-first rebuild — year three grind.
Georgia State.ot: Atlanta talent, run-heavy identity, developing QB room.

James Madison.ob: Billy Napier lands at the G5's best program — inheriting the roster Chesney took to the brink of the CFP.
James Madison.ot: Napier's process-driven pro-spread meets JMU's absurd G5 talent level; the fit is cleaner than Gainesville ever was.
James Madison.db: 4-2-5 loaded — the Sun Belt's best unit returns its core.
James Madison.dt: The +13.2 residual flagged them as market-underrated in 2025 and the CFP push proved it.
s: Best G5 roster east of Boise

Louisiana.ob: Desormeaux's option-influenced spread — the Cajuns' physical identity, retooling year.
Louisiana.ot: The -10.9 residual said the market overrated 2025; graduations across both lines make this a legitimate reset.
Louisiana.dt: League-median at best while the two-deep regrows.
w: Sun Belt West rising around them

Marshall.ob: Tony Gibson's defense-first program — year two after the title-game run.
Marshall.ot: Run-heavy, physical, QB-stable; the Herd's identity is set.
Marshall.db: 3-3-5 attacking — Gibson's specialty and the league's most disruptive front.
Marshall.dt: Top-third G5 havoc; they break offenses that lack answers vs pressure.

Old Dominion.ob: Rahne's spread around Colton Joseph — the league's best returning dual-threat.
Old Dominion.ot: Colton Joseph left for Wisconsin in the spring portal — the offense that was built entirely around him now holds an open competition (Jaxon Potter the veteran of the room). The system stays; the star is gone.
Old Dominion.dt: Good enough behind Joseph; not good enough without him.
w: Joseph exit removes the entire offensive identity

South Alabama.ob: Major Applewhite's balanced spread — steady, veteran, bowl-level.
South Alabama.ot: The -9.3 residual suggested market overrating in 2025; honest mid-tier now.

Southern Miss.ob: Blake Anderson promoted after Huff's Memphis exit — continuity for the league's fastest one-year turnaround.
Southern Miss.ot: The roster Huff rebuilt returns substantial production; Anderson's own HC resume (Arkansas State, USU) eases the handoff.
Southern Miss.dt: Top-half league after being bottom-five in 2024 — the flip was real.

Texas State.ob: GJ Kinne's tempo spread — the league's most explosive offense, now with expectations (and a new conference? verify Pac-12 move timing).
Texas State.ot: Kinne's system prints yards; the program's trajectory is the Sun Belt's steepest.
Texas State.dt: Median and climbing; the offense buys margin.
s: Kinne's offense elite for the level

Troy.ob: Gerad Parker's physical program — the Trojans' defensive identity persists.
Troy.ot: Run-first offense, veteran defense; November football wins the division historically.
Troy.dt: Top-third annually regardless of staff; it's cultural.

North Dakota State.ob: The FCS dynasty goes FBS — power run game, TE-heavy, the winningest program model in college football (verify 2026 staff).
North Dakota State.ot: The Bison bring championship infrastructure, not transition chaos; the roster out-executes its new league immediately even if athleticism lags.
North Dakota State.dt: No FPI prior exists; treat market lines on them with humility either way.

Sacramento State.ob: FBS newcomer with aggressive NIL ambition — the boldest transition bet of the cycle (verify staff).
Sacramento State.ot: The program spent like an FBS builder before arriving; roster quality is genuinely unknown against this level.

UConn.db: 4-2-5, structured — Candle's programs defend competently, always.

UL Monroe.ob: The league's hardest job — perennial rebuild, new energy annually (verify staff).
UL Monroe.ot: Roster assembled from portal overflow; competitive quarters are the metric.
UL Monroe.dt: Bottom-of-FBS metrics; every win is coaching.
